{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Nose Myōkenzan | image = [[File:Nose Myokenzan kaiunden.jpg|260px]]
Main Hall | location = 661 Nomachū, Nose-chō, Toyono-gun, Osaka, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34|55|44.328|N|135|27|59.245|E|scale:20000}} | mount = Murozan | sect = Nichiren | deity = Myōken Bosatsu | established = Keichō 8 (1603) | founder = Nitchi | main founder = Nose Yoritugu | alias = Nose Myōkendō
Nose Myōken-gū | official_name = Murozan Shinjō-ji Detached Temple Nose Myōkenzan | related_temples = [[Shinjō-ji (Nose)|Shinjō-ji]] | website = [https://www.myoken.org/ Official Website] }} '''Nose Myōkenzan''' (能勢妙見山, {{lang-en-short|Nose Myōkenzan}}) is a {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|日蓮宗}} temple located in Nose Town, Toyono District, Osaka, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Murozan. The main deity is {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}. Situated near the summit of {{ill|Myōken Mountain (Osaka and Hyōgo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}}, the temple is a revered site for the North Star faith. == Overview == '''Nose Myōkendō''' (能勢妙見堂) or '''Nose Myōken-gū''' (能勢妙見宮), as it is sometimes called, reflects the influence of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合}}, as evidenced by the presence of a {{ill|torii|ja|鳥居}} gate at the temple's entrance.{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.kawanishi.hyogo.jp/dbps_data/_material_/localhost/00test/myoukenryousen.pdf#page=3|title=妙見稜線コース（川西市ハイキングコース案内）|date=2018-03-06|accessdate=2024-10-23}} The temple’s full name is '''Murozan Shinjō-ji Detached Temple Nose Myōkenzan'''.{{Cite web|url=https://www.myoken.org/omen/|title=能勢妙見山の正式名称|publisher=日蓮宗霊場 能勢妙見山|accessdate=2024-10-23}} It is a {{ill|detached temple|ja|境外仏堂}} of {{ill|Shinjō-ji (Nose)|ja|真如寺}}, also located in Nose Town. Although technically subordinate to Shinjō-ji, Nose Myōkenzan attracts far more visitors and holds a significant position among Nichiren temples in the Kansai region. Initially managed directly by Shinjō-ji’s priests, it was designated as a detached temple under the {{ill|Religious Organizations Law (Japan)|ja|宗教団体法}} in 1941. Notably, a nearby temple, {{ill|Hontaki-ji|ja|本瀧寺}}, which also enshrines Myōken Bosatsu, is unrelated to Nose Myōkenzan and belongs to the {{ill|Myōken sect|ja|妙見宗}} of the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} school. == History == The origins of the temple date back to the {{ill|Tenpyō-shōhō|ja|天平勝宝}} era (749–757) when the monk Gyōki established {{ill|Daikū-ji|ja|大空寺}} on the summit of Iraku Mountain (now Myōken Mountain).{{Cite web|url=https://smtrc.jp/town-archives/city/toyonaka/p02.html|title=能勢氏ゆかりの日蓮宗霊場「能勢妙見山」|publisher=三井住友トラスト不動産|accessdate=2024-10-23}} During the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, in 986 (Kanna 2), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|ja|源満仲}}, a devout follower of Myōken Bosatsu, relocated an image of {{ill|Chintaku Reifu|lt=Chintaku Reifu Shin|ja|鎮宅霊符神}} (a representation of Myōken) to this site. Later, Mitsunaka’s grandson, {{ill|Minamoto no Yorikuni|ja|源頼国}}, settled in the area, established the Nose clan, and continued to venerate the deity. In 1581 (Tenshō 9), {{ill|Nose Yoritugu|ja|能勢頼次}}, after being defeated by {{ill|Akechi Mitsuhide|ja|明智光秀}}, retreated to Daikū-ji’s remains and built {{ill|Iraku Mountain Castle|ja|為楽山城}}. Following the {{ill|Honnō-ji Incident|ja|本能寺の変}}, Yoritugu joined {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}’s enemies and fled, losing his domain. In 1600 (Keichō 5), Yoritugu regained Nose under {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} and was reinstated as a Tokugawa vassal. Yoritugu became a devout Nichiren follower under {{ill|Nitchi|ja|日乾}}, the 21st chief abbot of {{ill|Minobusan Kuon-ji|ja|身延山久遠寺}}, and established Shinjō-ji in 1603 (Keichō 8). A new Myōken image was carved and enshrined in the newly constructed {{ill|Ka'un-den|ja|開運殿}} on the former castle site, renaming the mountain to Myōken Mountain. == Grounds == The temple features numerous notable structures: * '''Ka'un-den (Main Hall)''' – Rebuilt in 1787 (Tenmei 7) through the patronage of Nose Yoritada. * '''Seirei (Faith Hall)''' – Built in 1998, featuring a glass-floored worship area. * '''Sōshidō (Founder’s Hall)''' – Renovated in 1896. * '''Jōsuidō (Pure Water Hall)''' – Donated by 4th-generation Nakamura Utaemon. * '''Ema-dō (Votive Tablet Hall)''' – Built in 1797 (Kansei 8). * '''Treasure Hall''' – Built in 1922. * '''Statues of Nitchi and Nose Yoritugu'''. == Access == While historically accessible by {{ill|Nose Kaidō|ja|能勢街道}}, modern visitors must rely on private transport or hiking, as public transit access ceased in 2023. Parking is available near the summit, and seasonal traffic restrictions may apply during major events such as New Year’s visits. Shuttle services are provided for worshippers from nearby transit points upon prior arrangement. == Cultural Properties == * '''Natural Monuments (Osaka Prefecture and Kawanishi City):''' ** Beech forest on Myōken Mountain.{{Cite web|url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/guide/spot/8kt5o8vuo9/|title=妙見山のブナ林｜のせでん【能勢電鉄】|accessdate=2024-10-23|publisher=能勢電鉄}} == External Links == * [https://www.myoken.org/ Official Website] * {{Instagram|nosemyokenzan}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Nose Myōkenzan}} [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Nichiren-shū temples]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Osaka]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Hybrid shrines and temples]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Nose, Osaka]] [[de:Yatsushiro Shrine]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] ==References== {{reflist}} {{Myōken Faith}}[[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Bb]] [[fr:Nose Myōkenzan]] ==References== {{reflist}} [[Category:bump]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:bump 1-20]] [[ja:能勢妙見山 (日蓮宗)]]

Although technically subordinate to Shinjō-ji, Nose Myōkenzan attracts far more visitors and holds a significant position among Nichiren temples in the Kansai region. Initially managed directly by Shinjō-ji’s priests, it was designated as a detached temple under the {{ill|Religious Organizations Law (Japan)|ja|宗教団体法}} in 1941. Notably, a nearby temple, {{ill|Hontaki-ji|ja|本瀧寺}}, which also enshrines Myōken Bosatsu, is unrelated to Nose Myōkenzan and belongs to the {{ill|Myōken sect|ja|妙見宗}} of the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} school. == History == The origins of the temple date back to the {{ill|Tenpyō-shōhō|ja|天平勝宝}} era (749–757) when the monk Gyōki established {{ill|Daikū-ji|ja|大空寺}} on the summit of Iraku Mountain (now Myōken Mountain).{{Cite web|url=https://smtrc.jp/town-archives/city/toyonaka/p02.html|title=能勢氏ゆかりの日蓮宗霊場「能勢妙見山」|publisher=三井住友トラスト不動産|accessdate=2024-10-23}} During the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, in 986 (Kanna 2), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|ja|源満仲}}, a devout follower of Myōken Bosatsu, relocated an image of {{ill|Chintaku Reifu|lt=Chintaku Reifu Shin|ja|鎮宅霊符神}} (a representation of Myōken) to this site. Later, Mitsunaka’s grandson, {{ill|Minamoto no Yorikuni|ja|源頼国}}, settled in the area, established the Nose clan, and continued to venerate the deity. In 1581 (Tenshō 9), {{ill|Nose Yoritugu|ja|能勢頼次}}, after being defeated by {{ill|Akechi Mitsuhide|ja|明智光秀}}, retreated to Daikū-ji’s remains and built {{ill|Iraku Mountain Castle|ja|為楽山城}}. Following the {{ill|Honnō-ji Incident|ja|本能寺の変}}, Yoritugu joined {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}’s enemies and fled, losing his domain. In 1600 (Keichō 5), Yoritugu regained Nose under {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} and was reinstated as a Tokugawa vassal. Yoritugu became a devout Nichiren follower under {{ill|Nitchi|ja|日乾}}, the 21st chief abbot of {{ill|Minobusan Kuon-ji|ja|身延山久遠寺}}, and established Shinjō-ji in 1603 (Keichō 8). A new Myōken image was carved and enshrined in the newly constructed {{ill|Ka'un-den|ja|開運殿}} on the former castle site, renaming the mountain to Myōken Mountain. == Grounds == The temple features numerous notable structures: * '''Ka'un-den (Main Hall)''' – Rebuilt in 1787 (Tenmei 7) through the patronage of Nose Yoritada. * '''Seirei (Faith Hall)''' – Built in 1998, featuring a glass-floored worship area. * '''Sōshidō (Founder’s Hall)''' – Renovated in 1896. * '''Jōsuidō (Pure Water Hall)''' – Donated by 4th-generation Nakamura Utaemon. * '''Ema-dō (Votive Tablet Hall)''' – Built in 1797 (Kansei 8). * '''Treasure Hall''' – Built in 1922. * '''Statues of Nitchi and Nose Yoritugu'''. == Access == While historically accessible by {{ill|Nose Kaidō|ja|能勢街道}}, modern visitors must rely on private transport or hiking, as public transit access ceased in 2023. Parking is available near the summit, and seasonal traffic restrictions may apply during major events such as New Year’s visits. Shuttle services are provided for worshippers from nearby transit points upon prior arrangement. == Cultural Properties == * '''Natural Monuments (Osaka Prefecture and Kawanishi City):''' ** Beech forest on Myōken Mountain.{{Cite web|url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/guide/spot/8kt5o8vuo9/|title=妙見山のブナ林｜のせでん【能勢電鉄】|accessdate=2024-10-23|publisher=能勢電鉄}} == External Links == * [https://www.myoken.org/ Official Website] * {{Instagram|nosemyokenzan}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Nose Myōkenzan}} [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Nichiren-shū temples]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Osaka]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Hybrid shrines and temples]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Nose, Osaka]] [[de:Yatsushiro Shrine]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] ==References== {{reflist}} {{Myōken Faith}}[[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Bb]] [[fr:Nose Myōkenzan]] ==References== {{reflist}} [[Category:bump]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[ja:能勢妙見山 (日蓮宗)]]

Main Hall | location = 661 Nomachū, Nose-chō, Toyono-gun, Osaka, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34|55|44.328|N|135|27|59.245|E|scale:20000}} | mount = Murozan | sect = Nichiren | deity = Myōken Bosatsu | established = Keichō 8 (1603) | founder = Nitchi | main founder = Nose Yoritugu | alias = Nose Myōkendō
Nose Myōken-gū | official_name = Murozan Shinjō-ji Detached Temple Nose Myōkenzan | related_temples = [[Shinjō-ji (Nose)|Shinjō-ji]] | website = [https://www.myoken.org/ Official Website] }} '''Nose Myōkenzan''' (能勢妙見山, {{lang-en-short|Nose Myōkenzan}}) is a {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|日蓮宗}} temple located in Nose Town, Toyono District, Osaka, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Murozan. The main deity is {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}. Situated near the summit of {{ill|Myōken Mountain (Osaka and Hyōgo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}}, the temple is a revered site for the North Star faith. == Overview == '''Nose Myōkendō''' (能勢妙見堂) or '''Nose Myōken-gū''' (能勢妙見宮), as it is sometimes called, reflects the influence of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合}}, as evidenced by the presence of a {{ill|torii|ja|鳥居}} gate at the temple's entrance.{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.kawanishi.hyogo.jp/dbps_data/_material_/localhost/00test/myoukenryousen.pdf#page=3|title=妙見稜線コース（川西市ハイキングコース案内）|date=2018-03-06|accessdate=2024-10-23}} The temple’s full name is '''Murozan Shinjō-ji Detached Temple Nose Myōkenzan'''.{{Cite web|url=https://www.myoken.org/omen/|title=能勢妙見山の正式名称|publisher=日蓮宗霊場 能勢妙見山|accessdate=2024-10-23}} It is a {{ill|detached temple|ja|境外仏堂}} of {{ill|Shinjō-ji (Nose)|ja|真如寺}}, also located in Nose Town. Yoritugu became a devout Nichiren follower under {{ill|Nitchi|ja|日乾}}, the 21st chief abbot of {{ill|Minobusan Kuon-ji|ja|身延山久遠寺}}, and established Shinjō-ji in 1603 (Keichō 8). A new Myōken image was carved and enshrined in the newly constructed {{ill|Ka'un-den|ja|開運殿}} on the former castle site, renaming the mountain to Myōken Mountain. == Grounds == The temple features numerous notable structures: * '''Ka'un-den (Main Hall)''' – Rebuilt in 1787 (Tenmei 7) through the patronage of Nose Yoritada. * '''Seirei (Faith Hall)''' – Built in 1998, featuring a glass-floored worship area. * '''Sōshidō (Founder’s Hall)''' – Renovated in 1896. * '''Jōsuidō (Pure Water Hall)''' – Donated by 4th-generation Nakamura Utaemon. * '''Ema-dō (Votive Tablet Hall)''' – Built in 1797 (Kansei 8). * '''Treasure Hall''' – Built in 1922. * '''Statues of Nitchi and Nose Yoritugu'''. == Access == While historically accessible by {{ill|Nose Kaidō|ja|能勢街道}}, modern visitors must rely on private transport or hiking, as public transit access ceased in 2023. A new Myōken image was carved and enshrined in the newly constructed {{ill|Ka'un-den|ja|開運殿}} on the former castle site, renaming the mountain to Myōken Mountain. == Grounds == The temple features numerous notable structures: * '''Ka'un-den (Main Hall)''' – Rebuilt in 1787 (Tenmei 7) through the patronage of Nose Yoritada. * '''Seirei (Faith Hall)''' – Built in 1998, featuring a glass-floored worship area. * '''Sōshidō (Founder’s Hall)''' – Renovated in 1896. * '''Jōsuidō (Pure Water Hall)''' – Donated by 4th-generation Nakamura Utaemon. * '''Ema-dō (Votive Tablet Hall)''' – Built in 1797 (Kansei 8). * '''Treasure Hall''' – Built in 1922. * '''Statues of Nitchi and Nose Yoritugu'''. == Access == While historically accessible by {{ill|Nose Kaidō|ja|能勢街道}}, modern visitors must rely on private transport or hiking, as public transit access ceased in 2023. Parking is available near the summit, and seasonal traffic restrictions may apply during major events such as New Year’s visits. Shuttle services are provided for worshippers from nearby transit points upon prior arrangement. == Cultural Properties == * '''Natural Monuments (Osaka Prefecture and Kawanishi City):''' ** Beech forest on Myōken Mountain.{{Cite web|url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/guide/spot/8kt5o8vuo9/|title=妙見山のブナ林｜のせでん【能勢電鉄】|accessdate=2024-10-23|publisher=能勢電鉄}} == External Links == * revision.comment: adding religious affiliation to the ones with a broken religious affiliation section
revision.text:
{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Nose Myōkenzan | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Nose Myokenzan kaiunden.jpg|260px]]
Main Hall | location = 661 Nomachū, Nose-chō, Toyono-gun, Osaka, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34|55|44.328|N|135|27|59.245|E|scale:20000}} | mount = Murozan | sect = Nichiren | deity = Myōken Bosatsu | established = Keichō 8 (1603) | founder = Nitchi | main founder = Nose Yoritugu | alias = Nose Myōkendō
Nose Myōken-gū | official_name = Murozan Shinjō-ji Detached Temple Nose Myōkenzan | related_temples = [[Shinjō-ji (Nose)|Shinjō-ji]] | website = [https://www.myoken.org/ Official Website] }} '''Nose Myōkenzan''' (能勢妙見山, {{lang-en-short|Nose Myōkenzan}}) is a {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|日蓮宗}} temple located in Nose Town, Toyono District, Osaka, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Murozan. The main deity is {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}. Situated near the summit of {{ill|Myōken Mountain (Osaka and Hyōgo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}}, the temple is a revered site for the North Star faith. == Overview == '''Nose Myōkendō''' (能勢妙見堂) or '''Nose Myōken-gū''' (能勢妙見宮), as it is sometimes called, reflects the influence of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合}}, as evidenced by the presence of a {{ill|torii|ja|鳥居}} gate at the temple's entrance.{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.kawanishi.hyogo.jp/dbps_data/_material_/localhost/00test/myoukenryousen.pdf#page=3|title=妙見稜線コース（川西市ハイキングコース案内）|date=2018-03-06|accessdate=2024-10-23}} The temple’s full name is '''Murozan Shinjō-ji Detached Temple Nose Myōkenzan'''.{{Cite web|url=https://www.myoken.org/omen/|title=能勢妙見山の正式名称|publisher=日蓮宗霊場 能勢妙見山|accessdate=2024-10-23}} It is a {{ill|detached temple|ja|境外仏堂}} of {{ill|Shinjō-ji (Nose)|ja|真如寺}}, also located in Nose Town. Although technically subordinate to Shinjō-ji, Nose Myōkenzan attracts far more visitors and holds a significant position among Nichiren temples in the Kansai region. Initially managed directly by Shinjō-ji’s priests, it was designated as a detached temple under the {{ill|Religious Organizations Law (Japan)|ja|宗教団体法}} in 1941. Notably, a nearby temple, {{ill|Hontaki-ji|ja|本瀧寺}}, which also enshrines Myōken Bosatsu, is unrelated to Nose Myōkenzan and belongs to the {{ill|Myōken sect|ja|妙見宗}} of the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} school. == History == The origins of the temple date back to the {{ill|Tenpyō-shōhō|ja|天平勝宝}} era (749–757) when the monk Gyōki established {{ill|Daikū-ji|ja|大空寺}} on the summit of Iraku Mountain (now Myōken Mountain).{{Cite web|url=https://smtrc.jp/town-archives/city/toyonaka/p02.html|title=能勢氏ゆかりの日蓮宗霊場「能勢妙見山」|publisher=三井住友トラスト不動産|accessdate=2024-10-23}} During the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, in 986 (Kanna 2), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|ja|源満仲}}, a devout follower of Myōken Bosatsu, relocated an image of {{ill|Chintaku Reifu|lt=Chintaku Reifu Shin|ja|鎮宅霊符神}} (a representation of Myōken) to this site. Later, Mitsunaka’s grandson, {{ill|Minamoto no Yorikuni|ja|源頼国}}, settled in the area, established the Nose clan, and continued to venerate the deity. In 1581 (Tenshō 9), {{ill|Nose Yoritugu|ja|能勢頼次}}, after being defeated by {{ill|Akechi Mitsuhide|ja|明智光秀}}, retreated to Daikū-ji’s remains and built {{ill|Iraku Mountain Castle|ja|為楽山城}}. Following the {{ill|Honnō-ji Incident|ja|本能寺の変}}, Yoritugu joined {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}’s enemies and fled, losing his domain. In 1600 (Keichō 5), Yoritugu regained Nose under {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} and was reinstated as a Tokugawa vassal. Yoritugu became a devout Nichiren follower under {{ill|Nitchi|ja|日乾}}, the 21st chief abbot of {{ill|Minobusan Kuon-ji|ja|身延山久遠寺}}, and established Shinjō-ji in 1603 (Keichō 8). A new Myōken image was carved and enshrined in the newly constructed {{ill|Ka'un-den|ja|開運殿}} on the former castle site, renaming the mountain to Myōken Mountain. == Grounds == The temple features numerous notable structures: * '''Ka'un-den (Main Hall)''' – Rebuilt in 1787 (Tenmei 7) through the patronage of Nose Yoritada. * '''Seirei (Faith Hall)''' – Built in 1998, featuring a glass-floored worship area. * '''Sōshidō (Founder’s Hall)''' – Renovated in 1896. * '''Jōsuidō (Pure Water Hall)''' – Donated by 4th-generation Nakamura Utaemon. * '''Ema-dō (Votive Tablet Hall)''' – Built in 1797 (Kansei 8). * '''Treasure Hall''' – Built in 1922. * '''Statues of Nitchi and Nose Yoritugu'''. == Access == While historically accessible by {{ill|Nose Kaidō|ja|能勢街道}}, modern visitors must rely on private transport or hiking, as public transit access ceased in 2023. Parking is available near the summit, and seasonal traffic restrictions may apply during major events such as New Year’s visits. Shuttle services are provided for worshippers from nearby transit points upon prior arrangement. == Cultural Properties == * '''Natural Monuments (Osaka Prefecture and Kawanishi City):''' ** Beech forest on Myōken Mountain.{{Cite web|url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/guide/spot/8kt5o8vuo9/|title=妙見山のブナ林｜のせでん【能勢電鉄】|accessdate=2024-10-23|publisher=能勢電鉄}} == External Links == * [https://www.myoken.org/ Official Website] * {{Instagram|nosemyokenzan}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Nose Myōkenzan}} [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] completion categories]] ==References== {{reflist}} {{Myōken Faith}}[[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Bb]] [[fr:Nose Myōkenzan]] ==References== {{reflist}} [[Category:bump]] In 1600 (Keichō 5), Yoritugu regained Nose under {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} and was reinstated as a Tokugawa vassal. Yoritugu became a devout Nichiren follower under {{ill|Nitchi|ja|日乾}}, the 21st chief abbot of {{ill|Minobusan Kuon-ji|ja|身延山久遠寺}}, and established Shinjō-ji in 1603 (Keichō 8). A new Myōken image was carved and enshrined in the newly constructed {{ill|Ka'un-den|ja|開運殿}} on the former castle site, renaming the mountain to Myōken Mountain. == Grounds == The temple features numerous notable structures: * '''Ka'un-den (Main Hall)''' – Rebuilt in 1787 (Tenmei 7) through the patronage of Nose Yoritada. * '''Seirei (Faith Hall)''' – Built in 1998, featuring a glass-floored worship area. * '''Sōshidō (Founder’s Hall)''' – Renovated in 1896. * '''Jōsuidō (Pure Water Hall)''' – Donated by 4th-generation Nakamura Utaemon. * '''Ema-dō (Votive Tablet Hall)''' – Built in 1797 (Kansei 8). * '''Treasure Hall''' – Built in 1922. * '''Statues of Nitchi and Nose Yoritugu'''. == Access == While historically accessible by {{ill|Nose Kaidō|ja|能勢街道}}, modern visitors must rely on private transport or hiking, as public transit access ceased in 2023. Parking is available near the summit, and seasonal traffic restrictions may apply during major events such as New Year’s visits. Shuttle services are provided for worshippers from nearby transit points upon prior arrangement. == Cultural Properties == * '''Natural Monuments (Osaka Prefecture and Kawanishi City):''' ** Beech forest on Myōken Mountain.{{Cite web|url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/guide/spot/8kt5o8vuo9/|title=妙見山のブナ林｜のせでん【能勢電鉄】|accessdate=2024-10-23|publisher=能勢電鉄}} == External Links == * [https://www.myoken.org/ Official Website] * {{Instagram|nosemyokenzan}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Nose Myōkenzan}} [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Nose revision.text:
{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Nose Myōkenzan | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Nose Myokenzan kaiunden.jpg|260px]]
Main Hall | location = 661 Nomachū, Nose-chō, Toyono-gun, Osaka, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34|55|44.328|N|135|27|59.245|E|scale:20000}} | mount = Murozan | sect = Nichiren | deity = Myōken Bosatsu | established = Keichō 8 (1603) | founder = Nitchi | main founder = Nose Yoritugu | alias = Nose Myōkendō
Nose Myōken-gū | official_name = Murozan Shinjō-ji Detached Temple Nose Myōkenzan | related_temples = [[Shinjō-ji (Nose)|Shinjō-ji]] | website = [https://www.myoken.org/ Official Website] }} '''Nose Myōkenzan''' (能勢妙見山, {{lang-en-short|Nose Myōkenzan}}) is a {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|日蓮宗}} temple located in Nose Town, Toyono District, Osaka, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Murozan. The main deity is {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}. Situated near the summit of {{ill|Myōken Mountain (Osaka and Hyōgo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}}, the temple is a revered site for the North Star faith. == Overview == '''Nose Myōkendō''' (能勢妙見堂) or '''Nose Myōken-gū''' (能勢妙見宮), as it is sometimes called, reflects the influence of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合}}, as evidenced by the presence of a {{ill|torii|ja|鳥居}} gate at the temple's entrance.{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.kawanishi.hyogo.jp/dbps_data/_material_/localhost/00test/myoukenryousen.pdf#page=3|title=妙見稜線コース（川西市ハイキングコース案内）|date=2018-03-06|accessdate=2024-10-23}} The temple’s full name is '''Murozan Shinjō-ji Detached Temple Nose Myōkenzan'''.{{Cite web|url=https://www.myoken.org/omen/|title=能勢妙見山の正式名称|publisher=日蓮宗霊場 能勢妙見山|accessdate=2024-10-23}} It is a {{ill|detached temple|ja|境外仏堂}} of {{ill|Shinjō-ji (Nose)|ja|真如寺}}, also located in Nose Town. Although technically subordinate to Shinjō-ji, Nose Myōkenzan attracts far more visitors and holds a significant position among Nichiren temples in the Kansai region. Initially managed directly by Shinjō-ji’s priests, it was designated as a detached temple under the {{ill|Religious Organizations Law (Japan)|ja|宗教団体法}} in 1941. Notably, a nearby temple, {{ill|Hontaki-ji|ja|本瀧寺}}, which also enshrines Myōken Bosatsu, is unrelated to Nose Myōkenzan and belongs to the {{ill|Myōken sect|ja|妙見宗}} of the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} school. == History == The origins of the temple date back to the {{ill|Tenpyō-shōhō|ja|天平勝宝}} era (749–757) when the monk Gyōki established {{ill|Daikū-ji|ja|大空寺}} on the summit of Iraku Mountain (now Myōken Mountain).{{Cite web|url=https://smtrc.jp/town-archives/city/toyonaka/p02.html|title=能勢氏ゆかりの日蓮宗霊場「能勢妙見山」|publisher=三井住友トラスト不動産|accessdate=2024-10-23}} During the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, in 986 (Kanna 2), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|ja|源満仲}}, a devout follower of Myōken Bosatsu, relocated an image of {{ill|Chintaku Reifu|lt=Chintaku Reifu Shin|ja|鎮宅霊符神}} (a representation of Myōken) to this site. Later, Mitsunaka’s grandson, {{ill|Minamoto no Yorikuni|ja|源頼国}}, settled in the area, established the Nose clan, and continued to venerate the deity. In 1581 (Tenshō 9), {{ill|Nose Yoritugu|ja|能勢頼次}}, after being defeated by {{ill|Akechi Mitsuhide|ja|明智光秀}}, retreated to Daikū-ji’s remains and built {{ill|Iraku Mountain Castle|ja|為楽山城}}. Following the {{ill|Honnō-ji Incident|ja|本能寺の変}}, Yoritugu joined {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}’s enemies and fled, losing his domain. In 1600 (Keichō 5), Yoritugu regained Nose under {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} and was reinstated as a Tokugawa vassal. Yoritugu became a devout Nichiren follower under {{ill|Nitchi|ja|日乾}}, the 21st chief abbot of {{ill|Minobusan Kuon-ji|ja|身延山久遠寺}}, and established Shinjō-ji in 1603 (Keichō 8). A new Myōken image was carved and enshrined in the newly constructed {{ill|Ka'un-den|ja|開運殿}} on the former castle site, renaming the mountain to Myōken Mountain. == Grounds == The temple features numerous notable structures: * '''Ka'un-den (Main Hall)''' – Rebuilt in 1787 (Tenmei 7) through the patronage of Nose Yoritada. * '''Seirei (Faith Hall)''' – Built in 1998, featuring a glass-floored worship area. * '''Sōshidō (Founder’s Hall)''' – Renovated in 1896. * '''Jōsuidō (Pure Water Hall)''' – Donated by 4th-generation Nakamura Utaemon. * '''Ema-dō (Votive Tablet Hall)''' – Built in 1797 (Kansei 8). * '''Treasure Hall''' – Built in 1922. * '''Statues of Nitchi and Nose Yoritugu'''. == Access == While historically accessible by {{ill|Nose Kaidō|ja|能勢街道}}, modern visitors must rely on private transport or hiking, as public transit access ceased in 2023. Parking is available near the summit, and seasonal traffic restrictions may apply during major events such as New Year’s visits. Shuttle services are provided for worshippers from nearby transit points upon prior arrangement. == Cultural Properties == * '''Natural Monuments (Osaka Prefecture and Kawanishi City):''' ** Beech forest on Myōken Mountain.{{Cite web|url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/guide/spot/8kt5o8vuo9/|title=妙見山のブナ林｜のせでん【能勢電鉄】|accessdate=2024-10-23|publisher=能勢電鉄}} == External Links == * Nose Myōken-gū | official_name = Murozan Shinjō-ji Detached Temple Nose Myōkenzan | related_temples = [[Shinjō-ji (Nose)|Shinjō-ji]] | website = [https://www.myoken.org/ Official Website] }} '''Nose Myōkenzan''' (能勢妙見山, {{lang-en-short|Nose Myōkenzan}}) is a {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|日蓮宗}} temple located in Nose Town, Toyono District, Osaka, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Murozan. The main deity is {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}. Situated near the summit of {{ill|Myōken Mountain (Osaka and Hyōgo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}}, the temple is a revered site for the North Star faith. == Overview == '''Nose Myōkendō''' (能勢妙見堂) or '''Nose Myōken-gū''' (能勢妙見宮), as it is sometimes called, reflects the influence of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合}}, as evidenced by the presence of a {{ill|torii|ja|鳥居}} gate at the temple's entrance.{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.kawanishi.hyogo.jp/dbps_data/_material_/localhost/00test/myoukenryousen.pdf#page=3|title=妙見稜線コース（川西市ハイキングコース案内）|date=2018-03-06|accessdate=2024-10-23}} The temple’s full name is '''Murozan Shinjō-ji Detached Temple Nose Myōkenzan'''.{{Cite web|url=https://www.myoken.org/omen/|title=能勢妙見山の正式名称|publisher=日蓮宗霊場 能勢妙見山|accessdate=2024-10-23}} It is a {{ill|detached temple|ja|境外仏堂}} of {{ill|Shinjō-ji (Nose)|ja|真如寺}}, also located in Nose Town. Although technically subordinate to Shinjō-ji, Nose Myōkenzan attracts far more visitors and holds a significant position among Nichiren temples in the Kansai region. Initially managed directly by Shinjō-ji’s priests, it was designated as a detached temple under the {{ill|Religious Organizations Law (Japan)|ja|宗教団体法}} in 1941. Notably, a nearby temple, {{ill|Hontaki-ji|ja|本瀧寺}}, which also enshrines Myōken Bosatsu, is unrelated to Nose Myōkenzan and belongs to the {{ill|Myōken sect|ja|妙見宗}} of the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} school. == History == The origins of the temple date back to the {{ill|Tenpyō-shōhō|ja|天平勝宝}} era (749–757) when the monk Gyōki established {{ill|Daikū-ji|ja|大空寺}} on the summit of Iraku Mountain (now Myōken Mountain).{{Cite web|url=https://smtrc.jp/town-archives/city/toyonaka/p02.html|title=能勢氏ゆかりの日蓮宗霊場「能勢妙見山」|publisher=三井住友トラスト不動産|accessdate=2024-10-23}} During the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, in 986 (Kanna 2), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|ja|源満仲}}, a devout follower of Myōken Bosatsu, relocated an image of {{ill|Chintaku Reifu|lt=Chintaku Reifu Shin|ja|鎮宅霊符神}} (a representation of Myōken) to this site. Later, Mitsunaka’s grandson, {{ill|Minamoto no Yorikuni|ja|源頼国}}, settled in the area, established the Nose clan, and continued to venerate the deity. In 1581 (Tenshō 9), {{ill|Nose Yoritugu|ja|能勢頼次}}, after being defeated by {{ill|Akechi Mitsuhide|ja|明智光秀}}, retreated to Daikū-ji’s remains and built {{ill|Iraku Mountain Castle|ja|為楽山城}}. Following the {{ill|Honnō-ji Incident|ja|本能寺の変}}, Yoritugu joined {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}’s enemies and fled, losing his domain. In 1600 (Keichō 5), Yoritugu regained Nose under {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} and was reinstated as a Tokugawa vassal. Yoritugu became a devout Nichiren follower under {{ill|Nitchi|ja|日乾}}, the 21st chief abbot of {{ill|Minobusan Kuon-ji|ja|身延山久遠寺}}, and established Shinjō-ji in 1603 (Keichō 8). A new Myōken image was carved and enshrined in the newly constructed {{ill|Ka'un-den|ja|開運殿}} on the former castle site, renaming the mountain to Myōken Mountain. == Grounds == The temple features numerous notable structures: * '''Ka'un-den (Main Hall)''' – Rebuilt in 1787 (Tenmei 7) through the patronage of Nose Yoritada. * '''Seirei (Faith Hall)''' – Built in 1998, featuring a glass-floored worship area. * '''Sōshidō (Founder’s Hall)''' – Renovated in 1896. * '''Jōsuidō (Pure Water Hall)''' – Donated by 4th-generation Nakamura Utaemon. * '''Ema-dō (Votive Tablet Hall)''' – Built in 1797 (Kansei 8). * '''Treasure Hall''' – Built in 1922. * '''Statues of Nitchi and Nose Yoritugu'''. == Access == While historically accessible by {{ill|Nose Kaidō|ja|能勢街道}}, modern visitors must rely on private transport or hiking, as public transit access ceased in 2023. Parking is available near the summit, and seasonal traffic restrictions may apply during major events such as New Year’s visits. Shuttle services are provided for worshippers from nearby transit points upon prior arrangement. == Cultural Properties == * '''Natural Monuments (Osaka Prefecture and Kawanishi City):''' ** Beech forest on Myōken Mountain.{{Cite web|url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/guide/spot/8kt5o8vuo9/|title=妙見山のブナ林｜のせでん【能勢電鉄】|accessdate=2024-10-23|publisher=能勢電鉄}} == External Links == * {{日本の寺院 | 名称 = 能勢妙見山 | 画像 = [[ファイル:Nose Myokenzan kaiunden.jpg|260px]]
本堂 | 所在地 = [[大阪府]][[豊能郡]][[能勢町]]野間中661番地 | 位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|55|44.328|N|135|27|59.245|E|scale:20000}} | 山号 = 無漏山 | 宗派 = [[日蓮宗]] | 寺格 = [[真如寺 (大阪府能勢町)|真如寺]][[境外仏堂]] | 本尊 = [[妙見菩薩]] | 創建年 = [[慶長]]8年（[[1603年]]） | 開山 = [[日乾]] | 開基 = [[能勢頼次]] | 正式名 = 無漏山眞如寺 境外仏堂 能勢妙見山 | 別称 = 能勢妙見堂
能勢妙見宮 | 札所等 = | 文化財 = | 公式HP = https://www.myoken.org/ | 公式HP名 = 日蓮宗霊場 能勢妙見山 | 地図= }} [[ファイル:Nose Myokenzan Seirei.jpg|thumb|200px|信徒会館　星嶺]] '''能勢妙見山'''（のせみょうけんざん、{{lang-en-short|Nose Myōkenzan}}）は、[[大阪府]][[豊能郡]][[能勢町]]にある[[日蓮宗]]の[[寺院]]。[[山号]]は無漏山（むろさん）{{efn|○○山○○寺の表記のような[[寺号]]が付かない。}}。[[本尊]]は[[妙見菩薩]]。[[妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)|妙見山]]の山頂付近にあり、[[北極星]]信仰の聖地として知られる。 == 概要 == '''能勢妙見堂'''（のせみょうげんどう）、また[[神仏習合]]時代の名残で境内入口に[[鳥居]]があることなどから、'''能勢妙見宮'''（のせみょうげんぐう）{{WAP|pid=11051550|url=www.city.kawanishi.hyogo.jp/dbps_data/_material_/localhost/00test/myoukenryousen.pdf#page=3|title=妙見稜線コース（川西市ハイキングコース案内 2018年3月）|date=2018-03-06}}との通称もある。 正式には'''無漏山眞如寺 境外仏堂 能勢妙見山'''と言い{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.myoken.org/omen/|title=能勢妙見山の正式名称|publisher=日蓮宗霊場 能勢妙見山|accessdate=2023-09-28|archiveurl=|archivedate=|url-status=live}}、能勢町地黄にある[[真如寺 (大阪府能勢町)|真如寺]]の[[境外仏堂]]である。境外仏堂とは[[飛び地]]境内にある仏堂の意だが、本寺の真如寺よりもはるかに多くの参詣者を集めており、[[近畿地方|関西地区]]における日蓮宗の寺院等においては重要な位置を占める。開山以来、真如寺住職が併せて受持していたが、[[宗教団体法]]の規定に基づき、[[1941年]]（[[昭和]]16年）に真如寺所属の仏堂となった。なお、山腹にある[[本瀧寺]]は妙見菩薩を祀り寺紋も酷似しているが、能勢妙見山とは無関係な[[天台宗]]系の[[妙見宗]]の寺院である。 == 歴史 == 当山の歴史は[[天平勝宝]]年間（[[749年]]{{Cite web|和書|url=https://smtrc.jp/town-archives/city/toyonaka/p02.html|title=能勢氏ゆかりの日蓮宗霊場「能勢妙見山」|publisher=三井住友トラスト不動産|accessdate=2023-09-28|archiveurl=|archivedate=|url-status=live}} - [[757年]]）に、為楽山（いらくさん、現・妙見山）の山頂に[[行基]]が為楽山[[大空寺]]{{要曖昧さ回避|date=2024年10月}}を建立したことに始まるとされる。 [[平安時代]]の[[寛和]]2年（[[986年]]）、[[妙見菩薩]]を信仰していた[[源満仲]]が屋敷で祀っていた鎮宅霊符神像（[[妙見菩薩]]の別名）を当地へ[[wikt:遷座|遷座]]した。その後、満仲の孫である[[源頼国]]が能勢に移住して[[能勢氏]]を称し、この地の領主となると、当地の妙見菩薩を篤く信仰した。 [[天正]]9年（[[1581年]]）に[[明智光秀]]に攻められた[[能勢頼次]]が本拠地の[[丸山城 (摂津国能勢郡)|丸山城]]から当山に移り、山頂の大空寺の跡地に[[為楽山城]]（大空寺城）を築いて光秀軍と戦うが、やがて光秀に降伏した。しかし、[[本能寺の変]]で[[織田信長]]が光秀に殺されると、そのまま光秀の下にとどまっていた頼次は[[豊臣秀吉|羽柴秀吉]]の攻撃を受け、丸山城が落城した。為楽山城にいた頼次は城を脱出して[[備前国]]に逃亡したため能勢の領地を失ってしまった。 その後、頼次は[[徳川家康]]の家臣となり　[[慶長]]5年（[[1600年]]）の[[関ヶ原の戦い]]で活躍したことにより、再び能勢の地を領地として宛がわれ、頼次は能勢氏を[[江戸幕府]]の[[旗本]]として再興した[https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/guide/pdf/magazine/2010-aki-naka.pdf 秋を感じながら能勢氏のゆかりの地へ（能勢電鉄沿線情報誌）]。 頼次は[[日蓮宗]]の[[日乾]]（後の[[日蓮宗]]総本山である[[身延山]][[久遠寺]]21世）に帰依し、頼次自ら[[開山 (仏教)#開基|開基]]となり日乾を新たに開いた真如寺の[[開山 (仏教)|開山]]とした{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.myoken.org/history/|title=能勢氏と妙見山の歴史|publisher=日蓮宗霊場 能勢妙見山|accessdate=2023-09-28|archiveurl=|archivedate=|url-status=live}}。慶長8年（[[1603年]]）には日乾の手によって新たな妙見菩薩像が彫られるが、これを行基ゆかりの大空寺の趾であり、またかつての自城であった為楽山城の跡地に慶長10年（[[1605年]]）に建立した本殿（開運殿）に祀ることとした。こうして山の名も妙見山と呼ばれるようになった。これが現在の能勢妙見山である。 その後、能勢氏の外護（げご）のもと多くの参詣者が当山を訪れるようになり「能勢の妙見さん」として近畿のみならず全国的に名が知られるようになった。当時の賑わいは[[落語]]の[[不精の代参]]からもうかがい知れる。 [[明和]]5年（[[1768年]]）3月15日には妙見山の[[女人禁制]]が解かれ誰でも入山できるようになった{{Sfn|八田至行『能勢妙見山』|p=35}}。[[安永]]2年（[[1773年]]）には本殿（開運殿）の内拝が許され、さらなる賑わいを見せた。 翌安永3年（[[1774年]]）、[[能勢頼直]]は[[江戸]]の[[本所]]に下屋敷を拝領したため妙見菩薩を屋敷内に勧請したが、やがてそれを発展させて誰もが参詣できるようにし[[能勢妙見山別院|妙見山別院]]とした{{Sfn|八田至行『能勢妙見山』|p=37}}。火伏札である「能勢の黒札」は火事の多かった江戸の町で大いに流行した。妙見山別院は[[坂本龍馬]]や[[勝海舟]]親子も通っていたことで知られており、[[日本放送協会|NHK]]の[[大河ドラマ]]「[[龍馬伝]]」でも妙見山別院が紹介された。また、11代目能勢頼富の代のとき（[[天保]]11年{{Sfn|八田至行『能勢妙見山』|p=38}}）に、[[大坂]]・[[天満 (大阪市)|天満]]の[[夫婦橋|夫婦池]]跡地に建てられた能勢氏天満屋敷内に妙見堂を建立し、そこに能勢妙見山の御霊像が法華[[勧請]]された（現在は[[正善院 (大阪市)|正善院]]）{{WAP|pid=12556423|url=www.town.nose.osaka.jp/material/files/group/1/3103bunnkazai.pdf|title=女夫池の妙見さん（広報のせ 2019年3月号）|date=2023-02-09}}。 [[天明]]7年（[[1787年]]）、焼失していた本殿（開運殿）を頼直の寄進によって再興した{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.myoken.org/about/|title=13.開運殿（本殿）|publisher=日蓮宗霊場 能勢妙見山|accessdate=2023-09-28|archiveurl=|archivedate=|url-status=live}}。 == 境内 == * 本殿（開運殿） - [[天明]]7年（[[1787年]]）、[[能勢頼直]]により再建。 * 祖師堂（棲神殿） - [[1896年]]（[[明治]]29年）改修。 * 浄水堂 - 4代目[[中村歌右衛門 (4代目)|中村歌右衛門]]による寄進。 * 経堂 - [[寛政]]8年（[[1797年]]）8月、能勢頼直により建立。 * 絵馬堂 - 寛政8年（1797年）8月、能勢頼直により再建。 * 庫裡 - [[1975年]]（[[昭和]]50年）5月再建。 * 祥雲閣 - [[1902年]]（明治35年）建築。 * 鐘楼 * [[日蓮]]聖人像 * 山門 - 山門は[[大阪府]]と[[兵庫県]]の県境にある。 * 信徒会館「星嶺」 - [[1998年]]（[[平成]]10年）4月築。 * 宝物館 - [[1922年]]（[[大正]]11年）築。 * [[日乾]]上人像 * [[能勢頼次]]像 === 信徒会館「星嶺」 === 山頂付近には「星嶺」（せいれい）という、当山の寺紋（能勢氏の[[家紋]]である切竹矢筈十字）をかたどった信徒会館が[[1998年]]（[[平成]]10年）4月に建てられており、2階の[[礼拝堂]]は床が全て[[ガラス]]張りという[[仏教]]の礼拝堂とは思えない構造となっている。中には以前は寺院の売店があり、この売店は[[簡易郵便局]]も受託していた。能勢妙見宮の簡易郵便局受託解除により[[郵政民営化]]直前の[[2007年]]（平成19年）5月2日限りで一時閉鎖扱いとなった。その後[[2009年]]（平成21年）6月29日に、境内の北側にある[[特定郵便局]]であった元・妙見山郵便局の局舎にて委託者を変更して再開した。売店があった頃はいつでも自由に内部に入ることができ、無料休憩所もあったが、現在では毎月15日の月例法要や星嶺祭など特別行事の時以外は立ち入れなくなっており{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.myoken.org/seirei/|title=信徒会館「星嶺」について|publisher=日蓮宗霊場 能勢妙見山|accessdate=2023-09-28|archiveurl=|archivedate=|url-status=live}}、売店は寺務所の左手の廃業した旅館に移っている。設計は[[高松伸]]で、[[1999年]]に[[BCS賞]]を受賞している{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.nikkenren.com/kenchiku/bcs/result_year.html?from=search&year=1999|title=日建連表彰 BCS賞 第40回受賞作品（1999年）|publisher=[[日本建設業連合会]]|accessdate=2025-02-13}}{{Cite web|和書|url=https://data.shinkenchiku.online/projects/articles/SK_1998_07_104-0|title=能勢妙見山信徒会館 星嶺|website=[[新建築|新建築データ]]|publisher=[[新建築社|新建築データ]]|format=|accessdate=2025-02-13}}。 == 交通 == 当山への参道として[[能勢街道]]が、[[大阪市]]から[[池田市]]を経て当山まで古くから通じていた。また、[[能勢電鉄]]も元々は当山への参詣用に敷設されたものだった。2023年12月以降、妙見山へのアクセスは徒歩または自家用車のみとなっており、公共交通機関では直接アクセスできない。 山頂までは多くの旧参詣道（ハイキング道）がある。その多くが山麓から1時間以上かかるが、自家用車で訪れる場合の最も手軽なルートは[[本瀧寺]]から登るルートとされる。本瀧寺の本堂脇から登山道に入り、途中で廃寺の脇を通るなどして30分ほどで能勢妙見山本堂に到達できる。旧妙見山上停留所横に有料駐車場（1回500円）がある。 初日の出や初詣などで、大晦日から三が日にかけて例年非常に多くの参詣客が訪れるため、野間峠バス停（妙見荘交差点） - 清滝口交差点間は下り専用の一方通行となる。車で登る場合はバイパス経由のルートのみとなるなど、妙見山頂へ向かう主だった道路が一部一方通行にされることがある。 祈祷などの参拝者や奉賛会会員に限り、寺院側に事前予約をすれば、以下の3地点からの送迎が行われる。 *池田方面から阪急バス東能勢線「妙見口」停留所（豊能町野間口） *亀岡方面から亀岡市ふるさとバス「神地」停留所（亀岡市西別院町） *能勢電鉄[[妙見口駅]]（豊能町吉川） === バス（廃止） === かつては、[[阪急バス]]が[[茨木市駅|阪急茨木市駅]]・[[茨木駅|国鉄茨木駅]]から並びに[[内本町バスセンター|内本町二丁目]]・[[大阪梅田駅 (阪急)|梅田]]から、[[京都交通 (亀岡)|京都交通]]のバスが[[京都駅]]前（かつては[[祇園|京都祇園]]）・[[桂駅|阪急桂駅]]・[[亀岡駅|JR亀岡駅]]から運行されていたこともあった。茨木からの路線は1967年10月、大阪市街からの路線は1967年12月、京都市街からの路線は2005年4月に廃止された。 もっとも運行本数が多かった路線は、阪急バスによる[[阪急電鉄]][[阪急宝塚本線|宝塚線]]の[[池田駅 (大阪府)|池田駅]]からの路線であった。1997年12月に通年での直通運行が無くなった後も、2011年度までは[[正月三が日]]に直通運行があった。1997年12月に運行区間が短縮されて、余野（[[豊能町]]。池田駅や[[千里中央駅]]、[[茨木駅]]から路線あり） - 妙見山上を結ぶ阪急バス東能勢線137系統（日祝日のみ運行）が2019年7月7日の最終運行をもって（2019年7月13日のダイヤ改正で）廃止され、妙見山上へ乗り入れる路線バスはなくなった{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.hankyubus.co.jp/news/images/190703h.pdf |title=東能勢線の運行について |publisher=阪急バス |format=PDF |date=2019-07-03 |accessdate=2023-10-30}}。 2019年7月に廃止になった阪急バス「妙見山上」のバス停留所標識柱は、廃止後に寺院側の要望によって阪急バスから譲渡され、境内に場所を移して記念碑として保存されている。 === ケーブル・リフト（廃止） === 能勢電鉄[[能勢電鉄妙見線|妙見線]]の[[妙見口駅]]から徒歩約30分または[[阪急バス]]で「ケーブル黒川駅」停留所で、[[能勢電鉄妙見の森ケーブル|妙見の森ケーブル]][[黒川駅 (兵庫県)|黒川駅]]へ。ケーブルに乗り[[ケーブル山上駅 (兵庫県)|ケーブル山上駅]]で降車後、[[妙見の森リフト]]を乗継ぎ、徒歩300mで鳥居前へ到達した。 吉川（能勢電鉄妙見口駅） - ケーブル前間のバス路線は、[[京都交通 (亀岡)|京都交通]]による運行が行われていたが、2003年7月に廃止され、能勢町による運行助成のもとで阪急バスに引き継がれた。 正月三が日には、能勢電鉄などともに大晦日から元旦にかけて終夜運行されており、2019年まではこれに接続するバスも終夜運転されていた。 妙見の森ケーブル・妙見の森リフトは2023年12月3日を最終運行日として廃止された{{cite web |和書 |url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/guide/mountain_close/ |title=「妙見の森」営業終了のお知らせ |publisher=能勢電鉄 |accessdate=2024-10-23}}。 == 文化財 == === 大阪府・川西市指定天然記念物 === * 妙見山のブナ林{{Cite web|和書|url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/guide/spot/8kt5o8vuo9/|title=妙見山のブナ林｜のせでん【能勢電鉄】|accessdate=2019-08-27|publisher=能勢電鉄}} == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 注釈 === {{Notelist}} === 出典 === {{Reflist}} == 参考文献 == * {{Cite book|和書|author=八田至行|title=能勢妙見山――事蹟と信仰|date=1925-03|publisher=妙教会出版部|doi=10.11501/921022|ref={{SfnRef|八田至行『能勢妙見山』}}}} == 関連項目 == * [[妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)|能勢妙見山]] * [[真如寺 (大阪府能勢町)|真如寺]] == 外部リンク == {{Commons|Category:Nose Myokenzan}} * [https://www.myoken.org/ 日蓮宗霊場能勢妙見山公式HP] * {{Instagram|nosemyokenzan}} {{Buddhism-stub}} {{北摂山系の交通、名所}} {{DEFAULTSORT:のせみようけんさん}} [[Category:大阪府の寺]] [[Category:日蓮宗の寺院]] [[Category:神仏習合]] [[Category:修験道]] [[Category:能勢町の建築物]] [[Category:川西市の建築物]] [[Category:能勢町の歴史]] [[Category:川西市の歴史]] [[Category:県境]]

{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Nose Myōkenzan | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Nose Myokenzan kaiunden.jpg|260px]]
Main Hall | location = 661 Nomachū, Nose-chō, Toyono-gun, Osaka, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34|55|44.328|N|135|27|59.245|E|scale:20000}} | mount = Murozan | sect = Nichiren | deity = Myōken Bosatsu | established = Keichō 8 (1603) | founder = Nitchi | main founder = Nose Yoritugu | alias = Nose Myōkendō
Nose Myōken-gū | official_name = Murozan Shinjō-ji Detached Temple Nose Myōkenzan | related_temples = [[Shinjō-ji (Nose)|Shinjō-ji]] | website = [https://www.myoken.org/ Official Website] }} '''Nose Myōkenzan''' (能勢妙見山, {{lang-en-short|Nose Myōkenzan}}) is a {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|日蓮宗}} temple located in Nose Town, Toyono District, Osaka, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Murozan. The main deity is {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}. Situated near the summit of {{ill|Myōken Mountain (Osaka and Hyōgo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}}, the temple is a revered site for the North Star faith. == Overview == '''Nose Myōkendō''' (能勢妙見堂) or '''Nose Myōken-gū''' (能勢妙見宮), as it is sometimes called, reflects the influence of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合}}, as evidenced by the presence of a {{ill|torii|ja|鳥居}} gate at the temple's entrance.{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.kawanishi.hyogo.jp/dbps_data/_material_/localhost/00test/myoukenryousen.pdf#page=3|title=妙見稜線コース（川西市ハイキングコース案内）|date=2018-03-06|accessdate=2024-10-23}} The temple’s full name is '''Murozan Shinjō-ji Detached Temple Nose Myōkenzan'''.{{Cite web|url=https://www.myoken.org/omen/|title=能勢妙見山の正式name|publisher=日蓮宗霊場 能勢妙見山|accessdate=2024-10-23}} It is a {{ill|detached temple|ja|境外仏堂}} of {{ill|Shinjō-ji (Nose)|ja|真如寺}}, also located in Nose Town. Although technically subordinate to Shinjō-ji, Nose Myōkenzan attracts far more visitors and holds a significant position among Nichiren temples in the Kansai region. Initially managed directly by Shinjō-ji’s priests, it was designated as a detached temple under the {{ill|Religious Organizations Law (Japan)|ja|宗教団体法}} in 1941. Notably, a nearby temple, {{ill|Hontaki-ji|ja|本瀧寺}}, which also enshrines Myōken Bosatsu, is unrelated to Nose Myōkenzan and belongs to the {{ill|Myōken sect|ja|妙見宗}} of the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} school. == History == The origins of the temple date back to the {{ill|Tenpyō-shōhō|ja|天平勝宝}} era (749–757) when the monk Gyōki established {{ill|Daikū-ji|ja|大空寺}} on the summit of Iraku Mountain (now Myōken Mountain).{{Cite web|url=https://smtrc.jp/town-archives/city/toyonaka/p02.html|title=能勢氏ゆかりの日蓮宗霊場「能勢妙見山」|publisher=三井住友トラスト不動産|accessdate=2024-10-23}} During the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, in 986 (Kanna 2), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|ja|源満仲}}, a devout follower of Myōken Bosatsu, relocated an image of {{ill|Chintaku Reifu|lt=Chintaku Reifu Shin|ja|鎮宅霊符神}} (a representation of Myōken) to this site. Later, Mitsunaka’s grandson, {{ill|Minamoto no Yorikuni|ja|源頼国}}, settled in the area, established the Nose clan, and continued to venerate the deity. In 1581 (Tenshō 9), {{ill|Nose Yoritugu|ja|能勢頼次}}, after being defeated by {{ill|Akechi Mitsuhide|ja|明智光秀}}, retreated to Daikū-ji’s remains and built {{ill|Iraku Mountain Castle|ja|為楽山城}}. Following the {{ill|Honnō-ji Incident|ja|本能寺の変}}, Yoritugu joined {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}’s enemies and fled, losing his domain. In 1600 (Keichō 5), Yoritugu regained Nose under {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} and was reinstated as a Tokugawa vassal. Yoritugu became a devout Nichiren follower under {{ill|Nitchi|ja|日乾}}, the 21st chief abbot of {{ill|Minobusan Kuon-ji|ja|身延山久遠寺}}, and established Shinjō-ji in 1603 (Keichō 8). A new Myōken image was carved and enshrined in the newly constructed {{ill|Ka'un-den|ja|開運殿}} on the former castle site, renaming the mountain to Myōken Mountain. == Grounds == The temple features numerous notable structures: * '''Ka'un-den (Main Hall)''' – Rebuilt in 1787 (Tenmei 7) through the patronage of Nose Yoritada. * '''Seirei (Faith Hall)''' – Built in 1998, featuring a glass-floored worship area. * '''Sōshidō (Founder’s Hall)''' – Renovated in 1896. * '''Jōsuidō (Pure Water Hall)''' – Donated by 4th-generation Nakamura Utaemon. * '''Ema-dō (Votive Tablet Hall)''' – Built in 1797 (Kansei 8). * '''Treasure Hall''' – Built in 1922. * '''Statues of Nitchi and Nose Yoritugu'''. == Access == While historically accessible by {{ill|Nose Kaidō|ja|能勢街道}}, modern visitors must rely on private transport or hiking, as public transit access ceased in 2023. Parking is available near the summit, and seasonal traffic restrictions may apply during major events such as New Year’s visits. Shuttle services are provided for worshippers from nearby transit points upon prior arrangement. == Cultural Properties == * '''Natural Monuments (Osaka Prefecture and Kawanishi City):''' ** Beech forest on Myōken Mountain.{{Cite web|url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/guide/spot/8kt5o8vuo9/|title=妙見山のブナ林｜のせでん【能勢電鉄】|accessdate=2024-10-23|publisher=能勢電鉄}} == External Links == * [https://www.myoken.org/ Official Website] * {{Instagram|nosemyokenzan}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Nose Myōkenzan}} [[Category:Nose Myōkenzan]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Nose Myōkenzan]][[Category:Nichiren-shū temples]] [[Category:Nose Myōkenzan]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Nose Myōkenzan]][[Category:Buddhist temples in Osaka]] [[Category:Nose Myōkenzan]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Nose Myōkenzan]][[Category:Hybrid shrines and temples]] [[Category:Nose Myōkenzan]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] Myōkenzan]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Nose Myōkenzan]][[Category:Nose Myōkenzan]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Nose Myōkenzan]]==References== Nose Myōken-gū | official_name = Murozan Shinjō-ji Detached Temple Nose Myōkenzan | related_temples = [[Shinjō-ji (Nose)|Shinjō-ji]] | website = [https://www.myoken.org/ Official Website] }} '''Nose Myōkenzan''' (能勢妙見山, {{lang-en-short|Nose Myōkenzan}}) is a {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|日蓮宗}} temple located in Nose Town, Toyono District, Osaka, Japan. Although technically subordinate to Shinjō-ji, Nose Myōkenzan attracts far more visitors and holds a significant position among Nichiren temples in the Kansai region. Initially managed directly by Shinjō-ji’s priests, it was designated as a detached temple under the {{ill|Religious Organizations Law (Japan)|ja|宗教団体法}} in 1941. Notably, a nearby temple, {{ill|Hontaki-ji|ja|本瀧寺}}, which also enshrines Myōken Bosatsu, is unrelated to Nose Myōkenzan and belongs to the {{ill|Myōken sect|ja|妙見宗}} of the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} school. == History == The origins of the temple date back to the {{ill|Tenpyō-shōhō|ja|天平勝宝}} era (749–757) when the monk Gyōki established {{ill|Daikū-ji|ja|大空寺}} on the summit of Iraku Mountain (now Myōken Mountain).{{Cite web|url=https://smtrc.jp/town-archives/city/toyonaka/p02.html|title=能勢氏ゆかりの日蓮宗霊場「能勢妙見山」|publisher=三井住友トラスト不動産|accessdate=2024-10-23}} During the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, in 986 (Kanna 2), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|ja|源満仲}}, a devout follower of Myōken Bosatsu, relocated an image of {{ill|Chintaku Reifu|lt=Chintaku Reifu Shin|ja|鎮宅霊符神}} (a representation of Myōken) to this site. Later, Mitsunaka’s grandson, {{ill|Minamoto no Yorikuni|ja|源頼国}}, settled in the area, established the Nose clan, and continued to venerate the deity. In 1581 (Tenshō 9), {{ill|Nose Yoritugu|ja|能勢頼次}}, after being defeated by {{ill|Akechi Mitsuhide|ja|明智光秀}}, retreated to Daikū-ji’s remains and built {{ill|Iraku Mountain Castle|ja|為楽山城}}. Following the {{ill|Honnō-ji Incident|ja|本能寺の変}}, Yoritugu joined {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}’s enemies and fled, losing his domain. In 1600 (Keichō 5), Yoritugu regained Nose under {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} and was reinstated as a Tokugawa vassal. Yoritugu became a devout Nichiren follower under {{ill|Nitchi|ja|日乾}}, the 21st chief abbot of {{ill|Minobusan Kuon-ji|ja|身延山久遠寺}}, and established Shinjō-ji in 1603 (Keichō 8). A new Myōken image was carved and enshrined in the newly constructed {{ill|Ka'un-den|ja|開運殿}} on the former castle site, renaming the mountain to Myōken Mountain. == Grounds == The temple features numerous notable structures: * '''Ka'un-den (Main Hall)''' – Rebuilt in 1787 (Tenmei 7) through the patronage of Nose Yoritada. * '''Seirei (Faith Hall)''' – Built in 1998, featuring a glass-floored worship area. * '''Sōshidō (Founder’s Hall)''' – Renovated in 1896. * '''Jōsuidō (Pure Water Hall)''' – Donated by 4th-generation Nakamura Utaemon. * '''Ema-dō (Votive Tablet Hall)''' – Built in 1797 (Kansei 8). * '''Treasure Hall''' – Built in 1922. * '''Statues of Nitchi and Nose Yoritugu'''. == Access == While historically accessible by {{ill|Nose Kaidō|ja|能勢街道}}, modern visitors must rely on private transport or hiking, as public transit access ceased in 2023. Parking is available near the summit, and seasonal traffic restrictions may apply during major events such as New Year’s visits. Shuttle services are provided for worshippers from nearby transit points upon prior arrangement. == Cultural Properties == * '''Natural Monuments (Osaka Prefecture and Kawanishi City):''' ** Beech forest on Myōken Mountain.{{Cite web|url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/guide/spot/8kt5o8vuo9/|title=妙見山のブナ林｜のせでん【能勢電鉄】|accessdate=2024-10-23|publisher=能勢電鉄}} == External Links == * Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Murozan. The main deity is {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}. Situated near the summit of {{ill|Myōken Mountain (Osaka and Hyōgo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}}, the temple is a revered site for the North Star faith. == Overview == '''Nose Myōkendō''' (能勢妙見堂) or '''Nose Myōken-gū''' (能勢妙見宮), as it is sometimes called, reflects the influence of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合}}, as evidenced by the presence of a {{ill|torii|ja|鳥居}} gate at the temple's entrance.{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.kawanishi.hyogo.jp/dbps_data/_material_/localhost/00test/myoukenryousen.pdf#page=3|title=妙見稜線コース（川西市ハイキングコース案内）|date=2018-03-06|accessdate=2024-10-23}} The temple’s full name is '''Murozan Shinjō-ji Detached Temple Nose Myōkenzan'''.{{Cite web|url=https://www.myoken.org/omen/|title=能勢妙見山の正式name|publisher=日蓮宗霊場 能勢妙見山|accessdate=2024-10-23}} It is a {{ill|detached temple|ja|境外仏堂}} of {{ill|Shinjō-ji (Nose)|ja|真如寺}}, also located in Nose Town. Although technically subordinate to Shinjō-ji, Nose Myōkenzan attracts far more visitors and holds a significant position among Nichiren temples in the Kansai region. Initially managed directly by Shinjō-ji’s priests, it was designated as a detached temple under the {{ill|Religious Organizations Law (Japan)|ja|宗教団体法}} in 1941. Notably, a nearby temple, {{ill|Hontaki-ji|ja|本瀧寺}}, which also enshrines Myōken Bosatsu, is unrelated to Nose Myōkenzan and belongs to the {{ill|Myōken sect|ja|妙見宗}} of the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} school. == History == The origins of the temple date back to the {{ill|Tenpyō-shōhō|ja|天平勝宝}} era (749–757) when the monk Gyōki established {{ill|Daikū-ji|ja|大空寺}} on the summit of Iraku Mountain (now Myōken Mountain).{{Cite web|url=https://smtrc.jp/town-archives/city/toyonaka/p02.html|title=能勢氏ゆかりの日蓮宗霊場「能勢妙見山」|publisher=三井住友トラスト不動産|accessdate=2024-10-23}} During the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, in 986 (Kanna 2), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|ja|源満仲}}, a devout follower of Myōken Bosatsu, relocated an image of {{ill|Chintaku Reifu|lt=Chintaku Reifu Shin|ja|鎮宅霊符神}} (a representation of Myōken) to this site. Later, Mitsunaka’s grandson, {{ill|Minamoto no Yorikuni|ja|源頼国}}, settled in the area, established the Nose clan, and continued to venerate the deity. In 1581 (Tenshō 9), {{ill|Nose Yoritugu|ja|能勢頼次}}, after being defeated by {{ill|Akechi Mitsuhide|ja|明智光秀}}, retreated to Daikū-ji’s remains and built {{ill|Iraku Mountain Castle|ja|為楽山城}}. Following the {{ill|Honnō-ji Incident|ja|本能寺の変}}, Yoritugu joined {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}’s enemies and fled, losing his domain. In 1600 (Keichō 5), Yoritugu regained Nose under {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} and was reinstated as a Tokugawa vassal. Yoritugu became a devout Nichiren follower under {{ill|Nitchi|ja|日乾}}, the 21st chief abbot of {{ill|Minobusan Kuon-ji|ja|身延山久遠寺}}, and established Shinjō-ji in 1603 (Keichō 8). A new Myōken image was carved and enshrined in the newly constructed {{ill|Ka'un-den|ja|開運殿}} on the former castle site, renaming the mountain to Myōken Mountain. == Grounds == The temple features numerous notable structures: * '''Ka'un-den (Main Hall)''' – Rebuilt in 1787 (Tenmei 7) through the patronage of Nose Yoritada. * '''Seirei (Faith Hall)''' – Built in 1998, featuring a glass-floored worship area. * '''Sōshidō (Founder’s Hall)''' – Renovated in 1896. * '''Jōsuidō (Pure Water Hall)''' – Donated by 4th-generation Nakamura Utaemon. * '''Ema-dō (Votive Tablet Hall)''' – Built in 1797 (Kansei 8). * '''Treasure Hall''' – Built in 1922. * '''Statues of Nitchi and Nose Yoritugu'''. == Access == While historically accessible by {{ill|Nose Kaidō|ja|能勢街道}}, modern visitors must rely on private transport or hiking, as public transit access ceased in 2023. Parking is available near the summit, and seasonal traffic restrictions may apply during major events such as New Year’s visits. Shuttle services are provided for worshippers from nearby transit points upon prior arrangement. == Cultural Properties == * '''Natural Monuments (Osaka Prefecture and Kawanishi City):''' ** Beech forest on Myōken Mountain.{{Cite web|url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/guide/spot/8kt5o8vuo9/|title=妙見山のブナ林｜のせでん【能勢電鉄】|accessdate=2024-10-23|publisher=能勢電鉄}} == External Links == * {{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Nose Myōkenzan | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Nose Myokenzan kaiunden.jpg|260px]]
Main Hall | location = 661 Nomachū, Nose-chō, Toyono-gun, Osaka, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34|55|44.328|N|135|27|59.245|E|scale:20000}} | mount = Murozan | sect = Nichiren | deity = Myōken Bosatsu | established = Keichō 8 (1603) | founder = Nitchi | main founder = Nose Yoritugu | alias = Nose Myōkendō
Nose Myōken-gū | official_name = Murozan Shinjō-ji Detached Temple Nose Myōkenzan | related_temples = [[Shinjō-ji (Nose)|Shinjō-ji]] | website = [https://www.myoken.org/ Official Website] }} '''Nose Myōkenzan''' (能勢妙見山, {{lang-en-short|Nose Myōkenzan}}) is a {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|日蓮宗}} temple located in Nose Town, Toyono District, Osaka, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Murozan. The main deity is {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}. Situated near the summit of {{ill|Myōken Mountain (Osaka and Hyōgo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}}, the temple is a revered site for the North Star faith. == Overview == '''Nose Myōkendō''' (能勢妙見堂) or '''Nose Myōken-gū''' (能勢妙見宮), as it is sometimes called, reflects the influence of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合}}, as evidenced by the presence of a {{ill|torii|ja|鳥居}} gate at the temple's entrance.{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.kawanishi.hyogo.jp/dbps_data/_material_/localhost/00test/myoukenryousen.pdf#page=3|title=妙見稜線コース（川西市ハイキングコース案内）|date=2018-03-06|accessdate=2024-10-23}} The temple’s full name is '''Murozan Shinjō-ji Detached Temple Nose Myōkenzan'''.{{Cite web|url=https://www.myoken.org/omen/|title=能勢妙見山の正式name|publisher=日蓮宗霊場 能勢妙見山|accessdate=2024-10-23}} It is a {{ill|detached temple|ja|境外仏堂}} of {{ill|Shinjō-ji (Nose)|ja|真如寺}}, also located in Nose Town. Although technically subordinate to Shinjō-ji, Nose Myōkenzan attracts far more visitors and holds a significant position among Nichiren temples in the Kansai region. Initially managed directly by Shinjō-ji’s priests, it was designated as a detached temple under the {{ill|Religious Organizations Law (Japan)|ja|宗教団体法}} in 1941. Notably, a nearby temple, {{ill|Hontaki-ji|ja|本瀧寺}}, which also enshrines Myōken Bosatsu, is unrelated to Nose Myōkenzan and belongs to the {{ill|Myōken sect|ja|妙見宗}} of the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} school. == History == The origins of the temple date back to the {{ill|Tenpyō-shōhō|ja|天平勝宝}} era (749–757) when the monk Gyōki established {{ill|Daikū-ji|ja|大空寺}} on the summit of Iraku Mountain (now Myōken Mountain).{{Cite web|url=https://smtrc.jp/town-archives/city/toyonaka/p02.html|title=能勢氏ゆかりの日蓮宗霊場「能勢妙見山」|publisher=三井住友トラスト不動産|accessdate=2024-10-23}} During the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, in 986 (Kanna 2), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|ja|源満仲}}, a devout follower of Myōken Bosatsu, relocated an image of {{ill|Chintaku Reifu|lt=Chintaku Reifu Shin|ja|鎮宅霊符神}} (a representation of Myōken) to this site. Later, Mitsunaka’s grandson, {{ill|Minamoto no Yorikuni|ja|源頼国}}, settled in the area, established the Nose clan, and continued to venerate the deity. In 1581 (Tenshō 9), {{ill|Nose Yoritugu|ja|能勢頼次}}, after being defeated by {{ill|Akechi Mitsuhide|ja|明智光秀}}, retreated to Daikū-ji’s remains and built {{ill|Iraku Mountain Castle|ja|為楽山城}}. Following the {{ill|Honnō-ji Incident|ja|本能寺の変}}, Yoritugu joined {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}’s enemies and fled, losing his domain. In 1600 (Keichō 5), Yoritugu regained Nose under {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} and was reinstated as a Tokugawa vassal. Yoritugu became a devout Nichiren follower under {{ill|Nitchi|ja|日乾}}, the 21st chief abbot of {{ill|Minobusan Kuon-ji|ja|身延山久遠寺}}, and established Shinjō-ji in 1603 (Keichō 8). A new Myōken image was carved and enshrined in the newly constructed {{ill|Ka'un-den|ja|開運殿}} on the former castle site, renaming the mountain to Myōken Mountain. == Grounds == The temple features numerous notable structures: * '''Ka'un-den (Main Hall)''' – Rebuilt in 1787 (Tenmei 7) through the patronage of Nose Yoritada. * '''Seirei (Faith Hall)''' – Built in 1998, featuring a glass-floored worship area. * '''Sōshidō (Founder’s Hall)''' – Renovated in 1896. * '''Jōsuidō (Pure Water Hall)''' – Donated by 4th-generation Nakamura Utaemon. * '''Ema-dō (Votive Tablet Hall)''' – Built in 1797 (Kansei 8). * '''Treasure Hall''' – Built in 1922. * '''Statues of Nitchi and Nose Yoritugu'''. == Access == While historically accessible by {{ill|Nose Kaidō|ja|能勢街道}}, modern visitors must rely on private transport or hiking, as public transit access ceased in 2023. Parking is available near the summit, and seasonal traffic restrictions may apply during major events such as New Year’s visits. Shuttle services are provided for worshippers from nearby transit points upon prior arrangement. == Cultural Properties == * '''Natural Monuments (Osaka Prefecture and Kawanishi City):''' ** Beech forest on Myōken Mountain.{{Cite web|url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/guide/spot/8kt5o8vuo9/|title=妙見山のブナ林｜のせでん【能勢電鉄】|accessdate=2024-10-23|publisher=能勢電鉄}} == External Links == * {{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Nose Myōkenzan | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Nose Myokenzan kaiunden.jpg|260px]]
Main Hall | location = 661 Nomachū, Nose-chō, Toyono-gun, Osaka, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34|55|44.328|N|135|27|59.245|E|scale:20000}} | mount = Murozan | sect = Nichiren | deity = Myōken Bosatsu | established = Keichō 8 (1603) | founder = Nitchi | main founder = Nose Yoritugu | alias = Nose Myōkendō
Nose Myōken-gū | official_name = Murozan Shinjō-ji Detached Temple Nose Myōkenzan | related_temples = [[Shinjō-ji (Nose)|Shinjō-ji]] | website = [https://www.myoken.org/ Official Website] }} '''Nose Myōkenzan''' (能勢妙見山, {{lang-en-short|Nose Myōkenzan}}) is a {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|日蓮宗}} temple located in Nose Town, Toyono District, Osaka, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Murozan. The main deity is {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}. Situated near the summit of {{ill|Myōken Mountain (Osaka and Hyōgo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}}, the temple is a revered site for the North Star faith. == Overview == '''Nose Myōkendō''' (能勢妙見堂) or '''Nose Myōken-gū''' (能勢妙見宮), as it is sometimes called, reflects the influence of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合}}, as evidenced by the presence of a {{ill|torii|ja|鳥居}} gate at the temple's entrance.{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.kawanishi.hyogo.jp/dbps_data/_material_/localhost/00test/myoukenryousen.pdf#page=3|title=妙見稜線コース（川西市ハイキングコース案内）|date=2018-03-06|accessdate=2024-10-23}} The temple’s full name is '''Murozan Shinjō-ji Detached Temple Nose Myōkenzan'''.{{Cite web|url=https://www.myoken.org/omen/|title=能勢妙見山の正式name|publisher=日蓮宗霊場 能勢妙見山|accessdate=2024-10-23}} It is a {{ill|detached temple|ja|境外仏堂}} of {{ill|Shinjō-ji (Nose)|ja|真如寺}}, also located in Nose Town. Although technically subordinate to Shinjō-ji, Nose Myōkenzan attracts far more visitors and holds a significant position among Nichiren temples in the Kansai region. Initially managed directly by Shinjō-ji’s priests, it was designated as a detached temple under the {{ill|Religious Organizations Law (Japan)|ja|宗教団体法}} in 1941. Notably, a nearby temple, {{ill|Hontaki-ji|ja|本瀧寺}}, which also enshrines Myōken Bosatsu, is unrelated to Nose Myōkenzan and belongs to the {{ill|Myōken sect|ja|妙見宗}} of the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} school. == History == The origins of the temple date back to the {{ill|Tenpyō-shōhō|ja|天平勝宝}} era (749–757) when the monk Gyōki established {{ill|Daikū-ji|ja|大空寺}} on the summit of Iraku Mountain (now Myōken Mountain).{{Cite web|url=https://smtrc.jp/town-archives/city/toyonaka/p02.html|title=能勢氏ゆかりの日蓮宗霊場「能勢妙見山」|publisher=三井住友トラスト不動産|accessdate=2024-10-23}} During the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, in 986 (Kanna 2), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|ja|源満仲}}, a devout follower of Myōken Bosatsu, relocated an image of {{ill|Chintaku Reifu|lt=Chintaku Reifu Shin|ja|鎮宅霊符神}} (a representation of Myōken) to this site. Later, Mitsunaka’s grandson, {{ill|Minamoto no Yorikuni|ja|源頼国}}, settled in the area, established the Nose clan, and continued to venerate the deity. In 1581 (Tenshō 9), {{ill|Nose Yoritugu|ja|能勢頼次}}, after being defeated by {{ill|Akechi Mitsuhide|ja|明智光秀}}, retreated to Daikū-ji’s remains and built {{ill|Iraku Mountain Castle|ja|為楽山城}}. Following the {{ill|Honnō-ji Incident|ja|本能寺の変}}, Yoritugu joined {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}’s enemies and fled, losing his domain. In 1600 (Keichō 5), Yoritugu regained Nose under {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} and was reinstated as a Tokugawa vassal. Yoritugu became a devout Nichiren follower under {{ill|Nitchi|ja|日乾}}, the 21st chief abbot of {{ill|Minobusan Kuon-ji|ja|身延山久遠寺}}, and established Shinjō-ji in 1603 (Keichō 8). A new Myōken image was carved and enshrined in the newly constructed {{ill|Ka'un-den|ja|開運殿}} on the former castle site, renaming the mountain to Myōken Mountain. == Grounds == The temple features numerous notable structures: * '''Ka'un-den (Main Hall)''' – Rebuilt in 1787 (Tenmei 7) through the patronage of Nose Yoritada. * '''Seirei (Faith Hall)''' – Built in 1998, featuring a glass-floored worship area. * '''Sōshidō (Founder’s Hall)''' – Renovated in 1896. * '''Jōsuidō (Pure Water Hall)''' – Donated by 4th-generation Nakamura Utaemon. * '''Ema-dō (Votive Tablet Hall)''' – Built in 1797 (Kansei 8). * '''Treasure Hall''' – Built in 1922. * '''Statues of Nitchi and Nose Yoritugu'''. == Access == While historically accessible by {{ill|Nose Kaidō|ja|能勢街道}}, modern visitors must rely on private transport or hiking, as public transit access ceased in 2023. Parking is available near the summit, and seasonal traffic restrictions may apply during major events such as New Year’s visits. Shuttle services are provided for worshippers from nearby transit points upon prior arrangement. == Cultural Properties == * '''Natural Monuments (Osaka Prefecture and Kawanishi City):''' ** Beech forest on Myōken Mountain.{{Cite web|url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/guide/spot/8kt5o8vuo9/|title=妙見山のブナ林｜のせでん【能勢電鉄】|accessdate=2024-10-23|publisher=能勢電鉄}} == External Links == * In 1581 (Tenshō 9), {{ill|Nose Yoritugu|ja|能勢頼次}}, after being defeated by {{ill|Akechi Mitsuhide|ja|明智光秀}}, retreated to Daikū-ji’s remains and built {{ill|Iraku Mountain Castle|ja|為楽山城}}. Following the {{ill|Honnō-ji Incident|ja|本能寺の変}}, Yoritugu joined {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}’s enemies and fled, losing his domain. In 1600 (Keichō 5), Yoritugu regained Nose under {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} and was reinstated as a Tokugawa vassal. Yoritugu became a devout Nichiren follower under {{ill|Nitchi|ja|日乾}}, the 21st chief abbot of {{ill|Minobusan Kuon-ji|ja|身延山久遠寺}}, and established Shinjō-ji in 1603 (Keichō 8). A new Myōken image was carved and enshrined in the newly constructed {{ill|Ka'un-den|ja|開運殿}} on the former castle site, renaming the mountain to Myōken Mountain. == Grounds == The temple features numerous notable structures: * '''Ka'un-den (Main Hall)''' – Rebuilt in 1787 (Tenmei 7) through the patronage of Nose Yoritada. * '''Seirei (Faith Hall)''' – Built in 1998, featuring a glass-floored worship area. * '''Sōshidō (Founder’s Hall)''' – Renovated in 1896. * '''Jōsuidō (Pure Water Hall)''' – Donated by 4th-generation Nakamura Utaemon. * '''Ema-dō (Votive Tablet Hall)''' – Built in 1797 (Kansei 8). * '''Treasure Hall''' – Built in 1922. * '''Statues of Nitchi and Nose Yoritugu'''. == Access == While historically accessible by {{ill|Nose Kaidō|ja|能勢街道}}, modern visitors must rely on private transport or hiking, as public transit access ceased in 2023. Parking is available near the summit, and seasonal traffic restrictions may apply during major events such as New Year’s visits. Shuttle services are provided for worshippers from nearby transit points upon prior arrangement. == Cultural Properties == * '''Natural Monuments (Osaka Prefecture and Kawanishi City):''' ** Beech forest on Myōken Mountain.{{Cite web|url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/guide/spot/8kt5o8vuo9/|title=妙見山のブナ林｜のせでん【能勢電鉄】|accessdate=2024-10-23|publisher=能勢電鉄}} == External Links == * {{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Nose Myōkenzan | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Nose Myokenzan kaiunden.jpg|260px]]
Main Hall | location = 661 Nomachū, Nose-chō, Toyono-gun, Osaka, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34|55|44.328|N|135|27|59.245|E|scale:20000}} | mount = Murozan | sect = Nichiren | deity = Myōken Bosatsu | established = Keichō 8 (1603) | founder = Nitchi | main founder = Nose Yoritugu | alias = Nose Myōkendō
Nose Myōken-gū | official_name = Murozan Shinjō-ji Detached Temple Nose Myōkenzan | related_temples = [[Shinjō-ji (Nose)|Shinjō-ji]] | website = [https://www.myoken.org/ Official Website] }} '''Nose Myōkenzan''' (能勢妙見山, {{lang-en-short|Nose Myōkenzan}}) is a {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|日蓮宗}} temple located in Nose Town, Toyono District, Osaka, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Murozan. The main deity is {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}. Situated near the summit of {{ill|Myōken Mountain (Osaka and Hyōgo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}}, the temple is a revered site for the North Star faith. == Overview == '''Nose Myōkendō''' (能勢妙見堂) or '''Nose Myōken-gū''' (能勢妙見宮), as it is sometimes called, reflects the influence of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合}}, as evidenced by the presence of a {{ill|torii|ja|鳥居}} gate at the temple's entrance.{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.kawanishi.hyogo.jp/dbps_data/_material_/localhost/00test/myoukenryousen.pdf#page=3|title=妙見稜線コース（川西市ハイキングコース案内）|date=2018-03-06|accessdate=2024-10-23}} The temple’s full name is '''Murozan Shinjō-ji Detached Temple Nose Myōkenzan'''.{{Cite web|url=https://www.myoken.org/omen/|title=能勢妙見山の正式name|publisher=日蓮宗霊場 能勢妙見山|accessdate=2024-10-23}} It is a {{ill|detached temple|ja|境外仏堂}} of {{ill|Shinjō-ji (Nose)|ja|真如寺}}, also located in Nose Town. Although technically subordinate to Shinjō-ji, Nose Myōkenzan attracts far more visitors and holds a significant position among Nichiren temples in the Kansai region. Initially managed directly by Shinjō-ji’s priests, it was designated as a detached temple under the {{ill|Religious Organizations Law (Japan)|ja|宗教団体法}} in 1941. Notably, a nearby temple, {{ill|Hontaki-ji|ja|本瀧寺}}, which also enshrines Myōken Bosatsu, is unrelated to Nose Myōkenzan and belongs to the {{ill|Myōken sect|ja|妙見宗}} of the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} school. == History == The origins of the temple date back to the {{ill|Tenpyō-shōhō|ja|天平勝宝}} era (749–757) when the monk Gyōki established {{ill|Daikū-ji|ja|大空寺}} on the summit of Iraku Mountain (now Myōken Mountain).{{Cite web|url=https://smtrc.jp/town-archives/city/toyonaka/p02.html|title=能勢氏ゆかりの日蓮宗霊場「能勢妙見山」|publisher=三井住友トラスト不動産|accessdate=2024-10-23}} During the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, in 986 (Kanna 2), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|ja|源満仲}}, a devout follower of Myōken Bosatsu, relocated an image of {{ill|Chintaku Reifu|lt=Chintaku Reifu Shin|ja|鎮宅霊符神}} (a representation of Myōken) to this site. Later, Mitsunaka’s grandson, {{ill|Minamoto no Yorikuni|ja|源頼国}}, settled in the area, established the Nose clan, and continued to venerate the deity. In 1581 (Tenshō 9), {{ill|Nose Yoritugu|ja|能勢頼次}}, after being defeated by {{ill|Akechi Mitsuhide|ja|明智光秀}}, retreated to Daikū-ji’s remains and built {{ill|Iraku Mountain Castle|ja|為楽山城}}. Following the {{ill|Honnō-ji Incident|ja|本能寺の変}}, Yoritugu joined {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}’s enemies and fled, losing his domain. In 1600 (Keichō 5), Yoritugu regained Nose under {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} and was reinstated as a Tokugawa vassal. Yoritugu became a devout Nichiren follower under {{ill|Nitchi|ja|日乾}}, the 21st chief abbot of {{ill|Minobusan Kuon-ji|ja|身延山久遠寺}}, and established Shinjō-ji in 1603 (Keichō 8). A new Myōken image was carved and enshrined in the newly constructed {{ill|Ka'un-den|ja|開運殿}} on the former castle site, renaming the mountain to Myōken Mountain. == Grounds == The temple features numerous notable structures: * '''Ka'un-den (Main Hall)''' – Rebuilt in 1787 (Tenmei 7) through the patronage of Nose Yoritada. * '''Seirei (Faith Hall)''' – Built in 1998, featuring a glass-floored worship area. * '''Sōshidō (Founder’s Hall)''' – Renovated in 1896. * '''Jōsuidō (Pure Water Hall)''' – Donated by 4th-generation Nakamura Utaemon. * '''Ema-dō (Votive Tablet Hall)''' – Built in 1797 (Kansei 8). * '''Treasure Hall''' – Built in 1922. * '''Statues of Nitchi and Nose Yoritugu'''. == Access == While historically accessible by {{ill|Nose Kaidō|ja|能勢街道}}, modern visitors must rely on private transport or hiking, as public transit access ceased in 2023. Parking is available near the summit, and seasonal traffic restrictions may apply during major events such as New Year’s visits. Shuttle services are provided for worshippers from nearby transit points upon prior arrangement. == Cultural Properties == * '''Natural Monuments (Osaka Prefecture and Kawanishi City):''' ** Beech forest on Myōken Mountain.{{Cite web|url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/guide/spot/8kt5o8vuo9/|title=妙見山のブナ林｜のせでん【能勢電鉄】|accessdate=2024-10-23|publisher=能勢電鉄}} == External Links == * {{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Nose Myōkenzan | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Nose Myokenzan kaiunden.jpg|260px]]
Main Hall | location = 661 Nomachū, Nose-chō, Toyono-gun, Osaka, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34|55|44.328|N|135|27|59.245|E|scale:20000}} | mount = Murozan | sect = Nichiren | deity = Myōken Bosatsu | established = Keichō 8 (1603) | founder = Nitchi | main founder = Nose Yoritugu | alias = Nose Myōkendō
Nose Myōken-gū | official_name = Murozan Shinjō-ji Detached Temple Nose Myōkenzan | related_temples = [[Shinjō-ji (Nose)|Shinjō-ji]] | website = [https://www.myoken.org/ Official Website] }} '''Nose Myōkenzan''' (能勢妙見山, {{lang-en-short|Nose Myōkenzan}}) is a {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|日蓮宗}} temple located in Nose Town, Toyono District, Osaka, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Murozan. The main deity is {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}. Situated near the summit of {{ill|Myōken Mountain (Osaka and Hyōgo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}}, the temple is a revered site for the North Star faith. == Overview == '''Nose Myōkendō''' (能勢妙見堂) or '''Nose Myōken-gū''' (能勢妙見宮), as it is sometimes called, reflects the influence of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合}}, as evidenced by the presence of a {{ill|torii|ja|鳥居}} gate at the temple's entrance.{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.kawanishi.hyogo.jp/dbps_data/_material_/localhost/00test/myoukenryousen.pdf#page=3|title=妙見稜線コース（川西市ハイキングコース案内）|date=2018-03-06|accessdate=2024-10-23}} The temple’s full name is '''Murozan Shinjō-ji Detached Temple Nose Myōkenzan'''.{{Cite web|url=https://www.myoken.org/omen/|title=能勢妙見山の正式name|publisher=日蓮宗霊場 能勢妙見山|accessdate=2024-10-23}} It is a {{ill|detached temple|ja|境外仏堂}} of {{ill|Shinjō-ji (Nose)|ja|真如寺}}, also located in Nose Town. Although technically subordinate to Shinjō-ji, Nose Myōkenzan attracts far more visitors and holds a significant position among Nichiren temples in the Kansai region. Initially managed directly by Shinjō-ji’s priests, it was designated as a detached temple under the {{ill|Religious Organizations Law (Japan)|ja|宗教団体法}} in 1941. Notably, a nearby temple, {{ill|Hontaki-ji|ja|本瀧寺}}, which also enshrines Myōken Bosatsu, is unrelated to Nose Myōkenzan and belongs to the {{ill|Myōken sect|ja|妙見宗}} of the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} school. == History == The origins of the temple date back to the {{ill|Tenpyō-shōhō|ja|天平勝宝}} era (749–757) when the monk Gyōki established {{ill|Daikū-ji|ja|大空寺}} on the summit of Iraku Mountain (now Myōken Mountain).{{Cite web|url=https://smtrc.jp/town-archives/city/toyonaka/p02.html|title=能勢氏ゆかりの日蓮宗霊場「能勢妙見山」|publisher=三井住友トラスト不動産|accessdate=2024-10-23}} During the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, in 986 (Kanna 2), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|ja|源満仲}}, a devout follower of Myōken Bosatsu, relocated an image of {{ill|Chintaku Reifu|lt=Chintaku Reifu Shin|ja|鎮宅霊符神}} (a representation of Myōken) to this site. Later, Mitsunaka’s grandson, {{ill|Minamoto no Yorikuni|ja|源頼国}}, settled in the area, established the Nose clan, and continued to venerate the deity. In 1581 (Tenshō 9), {{ill|Nose Yoritugu|ja|能勢頼次}}, after being defeated by {{ill|Akechi Mitsuhide|ja|明智光秀}}, retreated to Daikū-ji’s remains and built {{ill|Iraku Mountain Castle|ja|為楽山城}}. Following the {{ill|Honnō-ji Incident|ja|本能寺の変}}, Yoritugu joined {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}’s enemies and fled, losing his domain. In 1600 (Keichō 5), Yoritugu regained Nose under {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} and was reinstated as a Tokugawa vassal. Yoritugu became a devout Nichiren follower under {{ill|Nitchi|ja|日乾}}, the 21st chief abbot of {{ill|Minobusan Kuon-ji|ja|身延山久遠寺}}, and established Shinjō-ji in 1603 (Keichō 8). A new Myōken image was carved and enshrined in the newly constructed {{ill|Ka'un-den|ja|開運殿}} on the former castle site, renaming the mountain to Myōken Mountain. == Grounds == The temple features numerous notable structures: * '''Ka'un-den (Main Hall)''' – Rebuilt in 1787 (Tenmei 7) through the patronage of Nose Yoritada. * '''Seirei (Faith Hall)''' – Built in 1998, featuring a glass-floored worship area. * '''Sōshidō (Founder’s Hall)''' – Renovated in 1896. * '''Jōsuidō (Pure Water Hall)''' – Donated by 4th-generation Nakamura Utaemon. * '''Ema-dō (Votive Tablet Hall)''' – Built in 1797 (Kansei 8). * '''Treasure Hall''' – Built in 1922. * '''Statues of Nitchi and Nose Yoritugu'''. == Access == While historically accessible by {{ill|Nose Kaidō|ja|能勢街道}}, modern visitors must rely on private transport or hiking, as public transit access ceased in 2023. Parking is available near the summit, and seasonal traffic restrictions may apply during major events such as New Year’s visits. Shuttle services are provided for worshippers from nearby transit points upon prior arrangement. == Cultural Properties == * '''Natural Monuments (Osaka Prefecture and Kawanishi City):''' ** Beech forest on Myōken Mountain.{{Cite web|url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/guide/spot/8kt5o8vuo9/|title=妙見山のブナ林｜のせでん【能勢電鉄】|accessdate=2024-10-23|publisher=能勢電鉄}} == External Links == * A new Myōken image was carved and enshrined in the newly constructed {{ill|Ka'un-den|ja|開運殿}} on the former castle site, renaming the mountain to Myōken Mountain. == Grounds == The temple features numerous notable structures: * '''Ka'un-den (Main Hall)''' – Rebuilt in 1787 (Tenmei 7) through the patronage of Nose Yoritada. * '''Seirei (Faith Hall)''' – Built in 1998, featuring a glass-floored worship area. * '''Sōshidō (Founder’s Hall)''' – Renovated in 1896. * '''Jōsuidō (Pure Water Hall)''' – Donated by 4th-generation Nakamura Utaemon. * '''Ema-dō (Votive Tablet Hall)''' – Built in 1797 (Kansei 8). * '''Treasure Hall''' – Built in 1922. * '''Statues of Nitchi and Nose Yoritugu'''. == Access == While historically accessible by {{ill|Nose Kaidō|ja|能勢街道}}, modern visitors must rely on private transport or hiking, as public transit access ceased in 2023. Parking is available near the summit, and seasonal traffic restrictions may apply during major events such as New Year’s visits. Shuttle services are provided for worshippers from nearby transit points upon prior arrangement. == Cultural Properties == * '''Natural Monuments (Osaka Prefecture and Kawanishi City):''' ** Beech forest on Myōken Mountain.{{Cite web|url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/guide/spot/8kt5o8vuo9/|title=妙見山のブナ林｜のせでん【能勢電鉄】|accessdate=2024-10-23|publisher=能勢電鉄}} == External Links == *
