{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Nose Myōkenzan | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Nose Myokenzan kaiunden.jpg|260px]]
Main Hall | location = 661 Nomachū, Nose-chō, Toyono-gun, Osaka, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34|55|44.328|N|135|27|59.245|E|scale:20000}} | mount = Murozan | sect = Nichiren | deity = Myōken Bosatsu | established = Keichō 8 (1603) | founder = Nitchi | main founder = Nose Yoritugu | alias = Nose Myōkendō
Nose Myōken-gū | official_name = Murozan Shinjō-ji Detached Temple Nose Myōkenzan | related_temples = [[Shinjō-ji (Nose)|Shinjō-ji]] | website = [https://www.myoken.org/ Official Website] }} '''Nose Myōkenzan''' (能勢妙見山, {{lang-en-short|Nose Myōkenzan}}) is a {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|日蓮宗}} temple located in Nose Town, Toyono District, Osaka, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Murozan. The main deity is {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}. Situated near the summit of {{ill|Myōken Mountain (Osaka and Hyōgo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}}, the temple is a revered site for the North Star faith. == Overview == '''Nose Myōkendō''' (能勢妙見堂) or '''Nose Myōken-gū''' (能勢妙見宮), as it is sometimes called, reflects the influence of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合}}, as evidenced by the presence of a {{ill|torii|ja|鳥居}} gate at the temple's entrance.{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.kawanishi.hyogo.jp/dbps_data/_material_/localhost/00test/myoukenryousen.pdf#page=3|title=妙見稜線コース（川西市ハイキングコース案内）|date=2018-03-06|accessdate=2024-10-23}} The temple’s full name is '''Murozan Shinjō-ji Detached Temple Nose Myōkenzan'''.{{Cite web|url=https://www.myoken.org/omen/|title=能勢妙見山の正式name|publisher=日蓮宗霊場 能勢妙見山|accessdate=2024-10-23}} It is a {{ill|detached temple|ja|境外仏堂}} of {{ill|Shinjō-ji (Nose)|ja|真如寺 (大阪府能勢町)|lt=Shinjō-ji}}, also located in Nose Town. Although technically subordinate to Shinjō-ji, Nose Myōkenzan attracts far more visitors and holds a significant position among Nichiren temples in the Kansai region. Initially managed directly by Shinjō-ji’s priests, it was designated as a detached temple under the {{ill|Religious Organizations Law (Japan)|ja|宗教団体法}} in 1941. Notably, a nearby temple, {{ill|Hontaki-ji|ja|本瀧寺}}, which also enshrines Myōken Bosatsu, is unrelated to Nose Myōkenzan and belongs to the {{ill|Myōken sect|ja|妙見宗}} of the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} school. == History == The origins of the temple date back to the {{ill|Tenpyō-shōhō|ja|天平勝宝}} era (749–757) when the monk Gyōki established {{ill|Daikū-ji|ja|大空寺}} on the summit of Iraku Mountain (now Myōken Mountain).{{Cite web|url=https://smtrc.jp/town-archives/city/toyonaka/p02.html|title=能勢氏ゆかりの日蓮宗霊場「能勢妙見山」|publisher=三井住友トラスト不動産|accessdate=2024-10-23}} During the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, in 986 (Kanna 2), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|ja|源満仲}}, a devout follower of Myōken Bosatsu, relocated an image of {{ill|Chintaku Reifu|lt=Chintaku Reifu Shin|ja|鎮宅霊符神}} (a representation of Myōken) to this site. Later, Mitsunaka’s grandson, {{ill|Minamoto no Yorikuni|ja|源頼国}}, settled in the area, established the Nose clan, and continued to venerate the deity. In 1581 (Tenshō 9), {{ill|Nose Yoritugu|ja|能勢頼次}}, after being defeated by {{ill|Akechi Mitsuhide|ja|明智光秀}}, retreated to Daikū-ji’s remains and built {{ill|Iraku Mountain Castle|ja|為楽山城}}. Following the {{ill|Honnō-ji Incident|ja|本能寺の変}}, Yoritugu joined {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}’s enemies and fled, losing his domain. In 1600 (Keichō 5), Yoritugu regained Nose under {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} and was reinstated as a Tokugawa vassal. Yoritugu became a devout Nichiren follower under {{ill|Nitchi|ja|日乾}}, the 21st chief abbot of {{ill|Minobusan Kuon-ji|ja|身延山久遠寺}}, and established Shinjō-ji in 1603 (Keichō 8). A new Myōken image was carved and enshrined in the newly constructed {{ill|Ka'un-den|ja|開運殿}} on the former castle site, renaming the mountain to Myōken Mountain. == Grounds == The temple features numerous notable structures: * '''Ka'un-den (Main Hall)''' – Rebuilt in 1787 (Tenmei 7) through the patronage of Nose Yoritada. * '''Seirei (Faith Hall)''' – Built in 1998, featuring a glass-floored worship area. * '''Sōshidō (Founder’s Hall)''' – Renovated in 1896. * '''Jōsuidō (Pure Water Hall)''' – Donated by 4th-generation Nakamura Utaemon. * '''Ema-dō (Votive Tablet Hall)''' – Built in 1797 (Kansei 8). * '''Treasure Hall''' – Built in 1922. * '''Statues of Nitchi and Nose Yoritugu'''. == Access == While historically accessible by {{ill|Nose Kaidō|ja|能勢街道}}, modern visitors must rely on private transport or hiking, as public transit access ceased in 2023. Parking is available near the summit, and seasonal traffic restrictions may apply during major events such as New Year’s visits. Shuttle services are provided for worshippers from nearby transit points upon prior arrangement. == Cultural Properties == * '''Natural Monuments (Osaka Prefecture and Kawanishi City):''' ** Beech forest on Myōken Mountain.{{Cite web|url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/guide/spot/8kt5o8vuo9/|title=妙見山のブナ林｜のせでん【能勢電鉄】|accessdate=2024-10-23|publisher=能勢電鉄}} == External Links == * Nose Myōken-gū | official_name = Murozan Shinjō-ji Detached Temple Nose Myōkenzan | related_temples = [[Shinjō-ji (Nose)|Shinjō-ji]] | website = [https://www.myoken.org/ Official Website] }} '''Nose Myōkenzan''' (能勢妙見山, {{lang-en-short|Nose Myōkenzan}}) is a {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|日蓮宗}} temple located in Nose Town, Toyono District, Osaka, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Murozan. The main deity is {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}. Situated near the summit of {{ill|Myōken Mountain (Osaka and Hyōgo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}}, the temple is a revered site for the North Star faith. == Overview == '''Nose Myōkendō''' (能勢妙見堂) or '''Nose Myōken-gū''' (能勢妙見宮), as it is sometimes called, reflects the influence of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合}}, as evidenced by the presence of a {{ill|torii|ja|鳥居}} gate at the temple's entrance.{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.kawanishi.hyogo.jp/dbps_data/_material_/localhost/00test/myoukenryousen.pdf#page=3|title=妙見稜線コース（川西市ハイキングコース案内）|date=2018-03-06|accessdate=2024-10-23}} The temple’s full name is '''Murozan Shinjō-ji Detached Temple Nose Myōkenzan'''.{{Cite web|url=https://www.myoken.org/omen/|title=能勢妙見山の正式name|publisher=日蓮宗霊場 能勢妙見山|accessdate=2024-10-23}} It is a {{ill|detached temple|ja|境外仏堂}} of {{ill|Shinjō-ji (Nose)|ja|真如寺 (大阪府能勢町)|lt=Shinjō-ji}}, also located in Nose Town. Although technically subordinate to Shinjō-ji, Nose Myōkenzan attracts far more visitors and holds a significant position among Nichiren temples in the Kansai region. Initially managed directly by Shinjō-ji’s priests, it was designated as a detached temple under the {{ill|Religious Organizations Law (Japan)|ja|宗教団体法}} in 1941. Notably, a nearby temple, {{ill|Hontaki-ji|ja|本瀧寺}}, which also enshrines Myōken Bosatsu, is unrelated to Nose Myōkenzan and belongs to the {{ill|Myōken sect|ja|妙見宗}} of the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} school. == History == The origins of the temple date back to the {{ill|Tenpyō-shōhō|ja|天平勝宝}} era (749–757) when the monk Gyōki established {{ill|Daikū-ji|ja|大空寺}} on the summit of Iraku Mountain (now Myōken Mountain).{{Cite web|url=https://smtrc.jp/town-archives/city/toyonaka/p02.html|title=能勢氏ゆかりの日蓮宗霊場「能勢妙見山」|publisher=三井住友トラスト不動産|accessdate=2024-10-23}} During the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, in 986 (Kanna 2), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|ja|源満仲}}, a devout follower of Myōken Bosatsu, relocated an image of {{ill|Chintaku Reifu|lt=Chintaku Reifu Shin|ja|鎮宅霊符神}} (a representation of Myōken) to this site. Later, Mitsunaka’s grandson, {{ill|Minamoto no Yorikuni|ja|源頼国}}, settled in the area, established the Nose clan, and continued to venerate the deity. In 1581 (Tenshō 9), {{ill|Nose Yoritugu|ja|能勢頼次}}, after being defeated by {{ill|Akechi Mitsuhide|ja|明智光秀}}, retreated to Daikū-ji’s remains and built {{ill|Iraku Mountain Castle|ja|為楽山城}}. Following the {{ill|Honnō-ji Incident|ja|本能寺の変}}, Yoritugu joined {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}’s enemies and fled, losing his domain. In 1600 (Keichō 5), Yoritugu regained Nose under {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} and was reinstated as a Tokugawa vassal. Yoritugu became a devout Nichiren follower under {{ill|Nitchi|ja|日乾}}, the 21st chief abbot of {{ill|Minobusan Kuon-ji|ja|身延山久遠寺}}, and established Shinjō-ji in 1603 (Keichō 8). A new Myōken image was carved and enshrined in the newly constructed {{ill|Ka'un-den|ja|開運殿}} on the former castle site, renaming the mountain to Myōken Mountain. == Grounds == The temple features numerous notable structures: * '''Ka'un-den (Main Hall)''' – Rebuilt in 1787 (Tenmei 7) through the patronage of Nose Yoritada. * '''Seirei (Faith Hall)''' – Built in 1998, featuring a glass-floored worship area. * '''Sōshidō (Founder’s Hall)''' – Renovated in 1896. * '''Jōsuidō (Pure Water Hall)''' – Donated by 4th-generation Nakamura Utaemon. * '''Ema-dō (Votive Tablet Hall)''' – Built in 1797 (Kansei 8). * '''Treasure Hall''' – Built in 1922. * '''Statues of Nitchi and Nose Yoritugu'''. == Access == While historically accessible by {{ill|Nose Kaidō|ja|能勢街道}}, modern visitors must rely on private transport or hiking, as public transit access ceased in 2023. Parking is available near the summit, and seasonal traffic restrictions may apply during major events such as New Year’s visits. Shuttle services are provided for worshippers from nearby transit points upon prior arrangement. == Cultural Properties == * '''Natural Monuments (Osaka Prefecture and Kawanishi City):''' ** Beech forest on Myōken Mountain.{{Cite web|url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/guide/spot/8kt5o8vuo9/|title=妙見山のブナ林｜のせでん【能勢電鉄】|accessdate=2024-10-23|publisher=能勢電鉄}} == External Links == * {{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Nose Myōkenzan | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Nose Myokenzan kaiunden.jpg|260px]]
Main Hall | location = 661 Nomachū, Nose-chō, Toyono-gun, Osaka, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34|55|44.328|N|135|27|59.245|E|scale:20000}} | mount = Murozan | sect = Nichiren | deity = Myōken Bosatsu | established = Keichō 8 (1603) | founder = Nitchi | main founder = Nose Yoritugu | alias = Nose Myōkendō
Nose Myōken-gū | official_name = Murozan Shinjō-ji Detached Temple Nose Myōkenzan | related_temples = [[Shinjō-ji (Nose)|Shinjō-ji]] | website = [https://www.myoken.org/ Official Website] }} '''Nose Myōkenzan''' (能勢妙見山, {{lang-en-short|Nose Myōkenzan}}) is a {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|日蓮宗}} temple located in Nose Town, Toyono District, Osaka, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Murozan. The main deity is {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}. Situated near the summit of {{ill|Myōken Mountain (Osaka and Hyōgo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}}, the temple is a revered site for the North Star faith. == Overview == '''Nose Myōkendō''' (能勢妙見堂) or '''Nose Myōken-gū''' (能勢妙見宮), as it is sometimes called, reflects the influence of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合}}, as evidenced by the presence of a {{ill|torii|ja|鳥居}} gate at the temple's entrance.{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.kawanishi.hyogo.jp/dbps_data/_material_/localhost/00test/myoukenryousen.pdf#page=3|title=妙見稜線コース（川西市ハイキングコース案内）|date=2018-03-06|accessdate=2024-10-23}} The temple’s full name is '''Murozan Shinjō-ji Detached Temple Nose Myōkenzan'''.{{Cite web|url=https://www.myoken.org/omen/|title=能勢妙見山の正式name|publisher=日蓮宗霊場 能勢妙見山|accessdate=2024-10-23}} It is a {{ill|detached temple|ja|境外仏堂}} of {{ill|Shinjō-ji (Nose)|ja|真如寺 (大阪府能勢町)|lt=Shinjō-ji}}, also located in Nose Town. Although technically subordinate to Shinjō-ji, Nose Myōkenzan attracts far more visitors and holds a significant position among Nichiren temples in the Kansai region. Initially managed directly by Shinjō-ji’s priests, it was designated as a detached temple under the {{ill|Religious Organizations Law (Japan)|ja|宗教団体法}} in 1941. Notably, a nearby temple, {{ill|Hontaki-ji|ja|本瀧寺}}, which also enshrines Myōken Bosatsu, is unrelated to Nose Myōkenzan and belongs to the {{ill|Myōken sect|ja|妙見宗}} of the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} school. == History == The origins of the temple date back to the {{ill|Tenpyō-shōhō|ja|天平勝宝}} era (749–757) when the monk Gyōki established {{ill|Daikū-ji|ja|大空寺}} on the summit of Iraku Mountain (now Myōken Mountain).{{Cite web|url=https://smtrc.jp/town-archives/city/toyonaka/p02.html|title=能勢氏ゆかりの日蓮宗霊場「能勢妙見山」|publisher=三井住友トラスト不動産|accessdate=2024-10-23}} During the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, in 986 (Kanna 2), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|ja|源満仲}}, a devout follower of Myōken Bosatsu, relocated an image of {{ill|Chintaku Reifu|lt=Chintaku Reifu Shin|ja|鎮宅霊符神}} (a representation of Myōken) to this site. Later, Mitsunaka’s grandson, {{ill|Minamoto no Yorikuni|ja|源頼国}}, settled in the area, established the Nose clan, and continued to venerate the deity. In 1581 (Tenshō 9), {{ill|Nose Yoritugu|ja|能勢頼次}}, after being defeated by {{ill|Akechi Mitsuhide|ja|明智光秀}}, retreated to Daikū-ji’s remains and built {{ill|Iraku Mountain Castle|ja|為楽山城}}. Following the {{ill|Honnō-ji Incident|ja|本能寺の変}}, Yoritugu joined {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}’s enemies and fled, losing his domain. In 1600 (Keichō 5), Yoritugu regained Nose under {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} and was reinstated as a Tokugawa vassal. Yoritugu became a devout Nichiren follower under {{ill|Nitchi|ja|日乾}}, the 21st chief abbot of {{ill|Minobusan Kuon-ji|ja|身延山久遠寺}}, and established Shinjō-ji in 1603 (Keichō 8). A new Myōken image was carved and enshrined in the newly constructed {{ill|Ka'un-den|ja|開運殿}} on the former castle site, renaming the mountain to Myōken Mountain. == Grounds == The temple features numerous notable structures: * '''Ka'un-den (Main Hall)''' – Rebuilt in 1787 (Tenmei 7) through the patronage of Nose Yoritada. * '''Seirei (Faith Hall)''' – Built in 1998, featuring a glass-floored worship area. * '''Sōshidō (Founder’s Hall)''' – Renovated in 1896. * '''Jōsuidō (Pure Water Hall)''' – Donated by 4th-generation Nakamura Utaemon. * '''Ema-dō (Votive Tablet Hall)''' – Built in 1797 (Kansei 8). * '''Treasure Hall''' – Built in 1922. * '''Statues of Nitchi and Nose Yoritugu'''. == Access == While historically accessible by {{ill|Nose Kaidō|ja|能勢街道}}, modern visitors must rely on private transport or hiking, as public transit access ceased in 2023. Parking is available near the summit, and seasonal traffic restrictions may apply during major events such as New Year’s visits. Shuttle services are provided for worshippers from nearby transit points upon prior arrangement. == Cultural Properties == * '''Natural Monuments (Osaka Prefecture and Kawanishi City):''' ** Beech forest on Myōken Mountain.{{Cite web|url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/guide/spot/8kt5o8vuo9/|title=妙見山のブナ林｜のせでん【能勢電鉄】|accessdate=2024-10-23|publisher=能勢電鉄}} == External Links == * [https://www.myoken.org/ Official Website] * {{Instagram|nosemyokenzan}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Nose Myōkenzan}} [[Category:Nose Myōkenzan]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq deprecated Later, Mitsunaka’s grandson, {{ill|Minamoto no Yorikuni|ja|源頼国}}, settled in the area, established the Nose clan, and continued to venerate the deity. In 1581 (Tenshō 9), {{ill|Nose Yoritugu|ja|能勢頼次}}, after being defeated by {{ill|Akechi Mitsuhide|ja|明智光秀}}, retreated to Daikū-ji’s remains and built {{ill|Iraku Mountain Castle|ja|為楽山城}}. Following the {{ill|Honnō-ji Incident|ja|本能寺の変}}, Yoritugu joined {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}’s enemies and fled, losing his domain. In 1600 (Keichō 5), Yoritugu regained Nose under {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} and was reinstated as a Tokugawa vassal. Yoritugu became a devout Nichiren follower under {{ill|Nitchi|ja|日乾}}, the 21st chief abbot of {{ill|Minobusan Kuon-ji|ja|身延山久遠寺}}, and established Shinjō-ji in 1603 (Keichō 8). A new Myōken image was carved and enshrined in the newly constructed {{ill|Ka'un-den|ja|開運殿}} on the former castle site, renaming the mountain to Myōken Mountain. == Grounds == The temple features numerous notable structures: * '''Ka'un-den (Main Hall)''' – Rebuilt in 1787 (Tenmei 7) through the patronage of Nose Yoritada. * '''Seirei (Faith Hall)''' – Built in 1998, featuring a glass-floored worship area. * '''Sōshidō (Founder’s Hall)''' – Renovated in 1896. * '''Jōsuidō (Pure Water Hall)''' – Donated by 4th-generation Nakamura Utaemon. * '''Ema-dō (Votive Tablet Hall)''' – Built in 1797 (Kansei 8). * '''Treasure Hall''' – Built in 1922. * '''Statues of Nitchi and Nose Yoritugu'''. == Access == While historically accessible by {{ill|Nose Kaidō|ja|能勢街道}}, modern visitors must rely on private transport or hiking, as public transit access ceased in 2023. Parking is available near the summit, and seasonal traffic restrictions may apply during major events such as New Year’s visits. Shuttle services are provided for worshippers from nearby transit points upon prior arrangement. == Cultural Properties == * '''Natural Monuments (Osaka Prefecture and Kawanishi City):''' ** Beech forest on Myōken Mountain.{{Cite web|url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/guide/spot/8kt5o8vuo9/|title=妙見山のブナ林｜のせでん【能勢電鉄】|accessdate=2024-10-23|publisher=能勢電鉄}} == External Links == * {{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Nose Myōkenzan | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Nose Myokenzan kaiunden.jpg|260px]]
Main Hall | location = 661 Nomachū, Nose-chō, Toyono-gun, Osaka, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34|55|44.328|N|135|27|59.245|E|scale:20000}} | mount = Murozan | sect = Nichiren | deity = Myōken Bosatsu | established = Keichō 8 (1603) | founder = Nitchi | main founder = Nose Yoritugu | alias = Nose Myōkendō
Nose Myōken-gū | official_name = Murozan Shinjō-ji Detached Temple Nose Myōkenzan | related_temples = [[Shinjō-ji (Nose)|Shinjō-ji]] | website = [https://www.myoken.org/ Official Website] }} '''Nose Myōkenzan''' (能勢妙見山, {{lang-en-short|Nose Myōkenzan}}) is a {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|日蓮宗}} temple located in Nose Town, Toyono District, Osaka, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Murozan. The main deity is {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}. Situated near the summit of {{ill|Myōken Mountain (Osaka and Hyōgo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}}, the temple is a revered site for the North Star faith. == Overview == '''Nose Myōkendō''' (能勢妙見堂) or '''Nose Myōken-gū''' (能勢妙見宮), as it is sometimes called, reflects the influence of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合}}, as evidenced by the presence of a {{ill|torii|ja|鳥居}} gate at the temple's entrance.{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.kawanishi.hyogo.jp/dbps_data/_material_/localhost/00test/myoukenryousen.pdf#page=3|title=妙見稜線コース（川西市ハイキングコース案内）|date=2018-03-06|accessdate=2024-10-23}} The temple’s full name is '''Murozan Shinjō-ji Detached Temple Nose Myōkenzan'''.{{Cite web|url=https://www.myoken.org/omen/|title=能勢妙見山の正式name|publisher=日蓮宗霊場 能勢妙見山|accessdate=2024-10-23}} It is a {{ill|detached temple|ja|境外仏堂}} of {{ill|Shinjō-ji (Nose)|ja|真如寺 (大阪府能勢町)|lt=Shinjō-ji}}, also located in Nose Town. Although technically subordinate to Shinjō-ji, Nose Myōkenzan attracts far more visitors and holds a significant position among Nichiren temples in the Kansai region. Initially managed directly by Shinjō-ji’s priests, it was designated as a detached temple under the {{ill|Religious Organizations Law (Japan)|ja|宗教団体法}} in 1941. Notably, a nearby temple, {{ill|Hontaki-ji|ja|本瀧寺}}, which also enshrines Myōken Bosatsu, is unrelated to Nose Myōkenzan and belongs to the {{ill|Myōken sect|ja|妙見宗}} of the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} school. == History == The origins of the temple date back to the {{ill|Tenpyō-shōhō|ja|天平勝宝}} era (749–757) when the monk Gyōki established {{ill|Daikū-ji|ja|大空寺}} on the summit of Iraku Mountain (now Myōken Mountain).{{Cite web|url=https://smtrc.jp/town-archives/city/toyonaka/p02.html|title=能勢氏ゆかりの日蓮宗霊場「能勢妙見山」|publisher=三井住友トラスト不動産|accessdate=2024-10-23}} During the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, in 986 (Kanna 2), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|ja|源満仲}}, a devout follower of Myōken Bosatsu, relocated an image of {{ill|Chintaku Reifu|lt=Chintaku Reifu Shin|ja|鎮宅霊符神}} (a representation of Myōken) to this site. Later, Mitsunaka’s grandson, {{ill|Minamoto no Yorikuni|ja|源頼国}}, settled in the area, established the Nose clan, and continued to venerate the deity. In 1581 (Tenshō 9), {{ill|Nose Yoritugu|ja|能勢頼次}}, after being defeated by {{ill|Akechi Mitsuhide|ja|明智光秀}}, retreated to Daikū-ji’s remains and built {{ill|Iraku Mountain Castle|ja|為楽山城}}. Following the {{ill|Honnō-ji Incident|ja|本能寺の変}}, Yoritugu joined {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}’s enemies and fled, losing his domain. In 1600 (Keichō 5), Yoritugu regained Nose under {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} and was reinstated as a Tokugawa vassal. Yoritugu became a devout Nichiren follower under {{ill|Nitchi|ja|日乾}}, the 21st chief abbot of {{ill|Minobusan Kuon-ji|ja|身延山久遠寺}}, and established Shinjō-ji in 1603 (Keichō 8). A new Myōken image was carved and enshrined in the newly constructed {{ill|Ka'un-den|ja|開運殿}} on the former castle site, renaming the mountain to Myōken Mountain. == Grounds == The temple features numerous notable structures: * '''Ka'un-den (Main Hall)''' – Rebuilt in 1787 (Tenmei 7) through the patronage of Nose Yoritada. * '''Seirei (Faith Hall)''' – Built in 1998, featuring a glass-floored worship area. * '''Sōshidō (Founder’s Hall)''' – Renovated in 1896. * '''Jōsuidō (Pure Water Hall)''' – Donated by 4th-generation Nakamura Utaemon. * '''Ema-dō (Votive Tablet Hall)''' – Built in 1797 (Kansei 8). * '''Treasure Hall''' – Built in 1922. * '''Statues of Nitchi and Nose Yoritugu'''. == Access == While historically accessible by {{ill|Nose Kaidō|ja|能勢街道}}, modern visitors must rely on private transport or hiking, as public transit access ceased in 2023. Parking is available near the summit, and seasonal traffic restrictions may apply during major events such as New Year’s visits. Shuttle services are provided for worshippers from nearby transit points upon prior arrangement. == Cultural Properties == * '''Natural Monuments (Osaka Prefecture and Kawanishi City):''' ** Beech forest on Myōken Mountain.{{Cite web|url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/guide/spot/8kt5o8vuo9/|title=妙見山のブナ林｜のせでん【能勢電鉄】|accessdate=2024-10-23|publisher=能勢電鉄}} == External Links == * {{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Nose Myōkenzan | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Nose Myokenzan kaiunden.jpg|260px]]
Main Hall | location = 661 Nomachū, Nose-chō, Toyono-gun, Osaka, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34|55|44.328|N|135|27|59.245|E|scale:20000}} | mount = Murozan | sect = Nichiren | deity = Myōken Bosatsu | established = Keichō 8 (1603) | founder = Nitchi | main founder = Nose Yoritugu | alias = Nose Myōkendō
Nose Myōken-gū | official_name = Murozan Shinjō-ji Detached Temple Nose Myōkenzan | related_temples = [[Shinjō-ji (Nose)|Shinjō-ji]] | website = [https://www.myoken.org/ Official Website] }} '''Nose Myōkenzan''' (能勢妙見山, {{lang-en-short|Nose Myōkenzan}}) is a {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|日蓮宗}} temple located in Nose Town, Toyono District, Osaka, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Murozan. The main deity is {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}. Situated near the summit of {{ill|Myōken Mountain (Osaka and Hyōgo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}}, the temple is a revered site for the North Star faith. == Overview == '''Nose Myōkendō''' (能勢妙見堂) or '''Nose Myōken-gū''' (能勢妙見宮), as it is sometimes called, reflects the influence of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合}}, as evidenced by the presence of a {{ill|torii|ja|鳥居}} gate at the temple's entrance.{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.kawanishi.hyogo.jp/dbps_data/_material_/localhost/00test/myoukenryousen.pdf#page=3|title=妙見稜線コース（川西市ハイキングコース案内）|date=2018-03-06|accessdate=2024-10-23}} The temple’s full name is '''Murozan Shinjō-ji Detached Temple Nose Myōkenzan'''.{{Cite web|url=https://www.myoken.org/omen/|title=能勢妙見山の正式name|publisher=日蓮宗霊場 能勢妙見山|accessdate=2024-10-23}} It is a {{ill|detached temple|ja|境外仏堂}} of {{ill|Shinjō-ji (Nose)|ja|真如寺 (大阪府能勢町)|lt=Shinjō-ji}}, also located in Nose Town. Although technically subordinate to Shinjō-ji, Nose Myōkenzan attracts far more visitors and holds a significant position among Nichiren temples in the Kansai region. Initially managed directly by Shinjō-ji’s priests, it was designated as a detached temple under the {{ill|Religious Organizations Law (Japan)|ja|宗教団体法}} in 1941. Notably, a nearby temple, {{ill|Hontaki-ji|ja|本瀧寺}}, which also enshrines Myōken Bosatsu, is unrelated to Nose Myōkenzan and belongs to the {{ill|Myōken sect|ja|妙見宗}} of the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} school. == History == The origins of the temple date back to the {{ill|Tenpyō-shōhō|ja|天平勝宝}} era (749–757) when the monk Gyōki established {{ill|Daikū-ji|ja|大空寺}} on the summit of Iraku Mountain (now Myōken Mountain).{{Cite web|url=https://smtrc.jp/town-archives/city/toyonaka/p02.html|title=能勢氏ゆかりの日蓮宗霊場「能勢妙見山」|publisher=三井住友トラスト不動産|accessdate=2024-10-23}} During the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, in 986 (Kanna 2), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|ja|源満仲}}, a devout follower of Myōken Bosatsu, relocated an image of {{ill|Chintaku Reifu|lt=Chintaku Reifu Shin|ja|鎮宅霊符神}} (a representation of Myōken) to this site. Later, Mitsunaka’s grandson, {{ill|Minamoto no Yorikuni|ja|源頼国}}, settled in the area, established the Nose clan, and continued to venerate the deity. In 1581 (Tenshō 9), {{ill|Nose Yoritugu|ja|能勢頼次}}, after being defeated by {{ill|Akechi Mitsuhide|ja|明智光秀}}, retreated to Daikū-ji’s remains and built {{ill|Iraku Mountain Castle|ja|為楽山城}}. Following the {{ill|Honnō-ji Incident|ja|本能寺の変}}, Yoritugu joined {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}’s enemies and fled, losing his domain. In 1600 (Keichō 5), Yoritugu regained Nose under {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} and was reinstated as a Tokugawa vassal. Yoritugu became a devout Nichiren follower under {{ill|Nitchi|ja|日乾}}, the 21st chief abbot of {{ill|Minobusan Kuon-ji|ja|身延山久遠寺}}, and established Shinjō-ji in 1603 (Keichō 8). A new Myōken image was carved and enshrined in the newly constructed {{ill|Ka'un-den|ja|開運殿}} on the former castle site, renaming the mountain to Myōken Mountain. == Grounds == The temple features numerous notable structures: * '''Ka'un-den (Main Hall)''' – Rebuilt in 1787 (Tenmei 7) through the patronage of Nose Yoritada. * '''Seirei (Faith Hall)''' – Built in 1998, featuring a glass-floored worship area. * '''Sōshidō (Founder’s Hall)''' – Renovated in 1896. * '''Jōsuidō (Pure Water Hall)''' – Donated by 4th-generation Nakamura Utaemon. * '''Ema-dō (Votive Tablet Hall)''' – Built in 1797 (Kansei 8). * '''Treasure Hall''' – Built in 1922. * '''Statues of Nitchi and Nose Yoritugu'''. == Access == While historically accessible by {{ill|Nose Kaidō|ja|能勢街道}}, modern visitors must rely on private transport or hiking, as public transit access ceased in 2023. Parking is available near the summit, and seasonal traffic restrictions may apply during major events such as New Year’s visits. Shuttle services are provided for worshippers from nearby transit points upon prior arrangement. == Cultural Properties == * '''Natural Monuments (Osaka Prefecture and Kawanishi City):''' ** Beech forest on Myōken Mountain.{{Cite web|url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/guide/spot/8kt5o8vuo9/|title=妙見山のブナ林｜のせでん【能勢電鉄】|accessdate=2024-10-23|publisher=能勢電鉄}} == External Links == * revision.text:
{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Nose Myōkenzan | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Nose Myokenzan kaiunden.jpg|260px]]
Main Hall | location = 661 Nomachū, Nose-chō, Toyono-gun, Osaka, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34|55|44.328|N|135|27|59.245|E|scale:20000}} | mount = Murozan | sect = Nichiren | deity = Myōken Bosatsu | established = Keichō 8 (1603) | founder = Nitchi | main founder = Nose Yoritugu | alias = Nose Myōkendō
Nose Myōken-gū | official_name = Murozan Shinjō-ji Detached Temple Nose Myōkenzan | related_temples = [[Shinjō-ji (Nose)|Shinjō-ji]] | website = [https://www.myoken.org/ Official Website] }} '''Nose Myōkenzan''' (能勢妙見山, {{lang-en-short|Nose Myōkenzan}}) is a {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|日蓮宗}} temple located in Nose Town, Toyono District, Osaka, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Murozan. The main deity is {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}. Situated near the summit of {{ill|Myōken Mountain (Osaka and Hyōgo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}}, the temple is a revered site for the North Star faith. == Overview == '''Nose Myōkendō''' (能勢妙見堂) or '''Nose Myōken-gū''' (能勢妙見宮), as it is sometimes called, reflects the influence of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合}}, as evidenced by the presence of a {{ill|torii|ja|鳥居}} gate at the temple's entrance.{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.kawanishi.hyogo.jp/dbps_data/_material_/localhost/00test/myoukenryousen.pdf#page=3|title=妙見稜線コース（川西市ハイキングコース案内）|date=2018-03-06|accessdate=2024-10-23}} The temple’s full name is '''Murozan Shinjō-ji Detached Temple Nose Myōkenzan'''.{{Cite web|url=https://www.myoken.org/omen/|title=能勢妙見山の正式name|publisher=日蓮宗霊場 能勢妙見山|accessdate=2024-10-23}} It is a {{ill|detached temple|ja|境外仏堂}} of {{ill|Shinjō-ji (Nose)|ja|真如寺 (大阪府能勢町)|lt=Shinjō-ji}}, also located in Nose Town. Although technically subordinate to Shinjō-ji, Nose Myōkenzan attracts far more visitors and holds a significant position among Nichiren temples in the Kansai region. Initially managed directly by Shinjō-ji’s priests, it was designated as a detached temple under the {{ill|Religious Organizations Law (Japan)|ja|宗教団体法}} in 1941. Notably, a nearby temple, {{ill|Hontaki-ji|ja|本瀧寺}}, which also enshrines Myōken Bosatsu, is unrelated to Nose Myōkenzan and belongs to the {{ill|Myōken sect|ja|妙見宗}} of the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} school. == History == The origins of the temple date back to the {{ill|Tenpyō-shōhō|ja|天平勝宝}} era (749–757) when the monk Gyōki established {{ill|Daikū-ji|ja|大空寺}} on the summit of Iraku Mountain (now Myōken Mountain).{{Cite web|url=https://smtrc.jp/town-archives/city/toyonaka/p02.html|title=能勢氏ゆかりの日蓮宗霊場「能勢妙見山」|publisher=三井住友トラスト不動産|accessdate=2024-10-23}} During the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, in 986 (Kanna 2), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|ja|源満仲}}, a devout follower of Myōken Bosatsu, relocated an image of {{ill|Chintaku Reifu|lt=Chintaku Reifu Shin|ja|鎮宅霊符神}} (a representation of Myōken) to this site. Later, Mitsunaka’s grandson, {{ill|Minamoto no Yorikuni|ja|源頼国}}, settled in the area, established the Nose clan, and continued to venerate the deity. In 1581 (Tenshō 9), {{ill|Nose Yoritugu|ja|能勢頼次}}, after being defeated by {{ill|Akechi Mitsuhide|ja|明智光秀}}, retreated to Daikū-ji’s remains and built {{ill|Iraku Mountain Castle|ja|為楽山城}}. Following the {{ill|Honnō-ji Incident|ja|本能寺の変}}, Yoritugu joined {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}’s enemies and fled, losing his domain. In 1600 (Keichō 5), Yoritugu regained Nose under {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} and was reinstated as a Tokugawa vassal. Yoritugu became a devout Nichiren follower under {{ill|Nitchi|ja|日乾}}, the 21st chief abbot of {{ill|Minobusan Kuon-ji|ja|身延山久遠寺}}, and established Shinjō-ji in 1603 (Keichō 8). A new Myōken image was carved and enshrined in the newly constructed {{ill|Ka'un-den|ja|開運殿}} on the former castle site, renaming the mountain to Myōken Mountain. == Grounds == The temple features numerous notable structures: * '''Ka'un-den (Main Hall)''' – Rebuilt in 1787 (Tenmei 7) through the patronage of Nose Yoritada. * '''Seirei (Faith Hall)''' – Built in 1998, featuring a glass-floored worship area. * '''Sōshidō (Founder’s Hall)''' – Renovated in 1896. * '''Jōsuidō (Pure Water Hall)''' – Donated by 4th-generation Nakamura Utaemon. * '''Ema-dō (Votive Tablet Hall)''' – Built in 1797 (Kansei 8). * '''Treasure Hall''' – Built in 1922. * '''Statues of Nitchi and Nose Yoritugu'''. == Access == While historically accessible by {{ill|Nose Kaidō|ja|能勢街道}}, modern visitors must rely on private transport or hiking, as public transit access ceased in 2023. Parking is available near the summit, and seasonal traffic restrictions may apply during major events such as New Year’s visits. Shuttle services are provided for worshippers from nearby transit points upon prior arrangement. == Cultural Properties == * '''Natural Monuments (Osaka Prefecture and Kawanishi City):''' ** Beech forest on Myōken Mountain.{{Cite web|url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/guide/spot/8kt5o8vuo9/|title=妙見山のブナ林｜のせでん【能勢電鉄】|accessdate=2024-10-23|publisher=能勢電鉄}} == External Links == * [https://www.myoken.org/ Official Website] * {{Instagram|nosemyokenzan}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Nose Myōkenzan}} [[Category:Nose Myōkenzan]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Myōkenzan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Nose Myōkenzan]][[Category:Nichiren-shū temples]] [[Category:Nose Myōkenzan]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Myōkenzan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Nose [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Nose Myōkenzan]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:suQqqq]] [[Category:suQqqq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Myōkenzan]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Myōkenzan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Myōkenzan]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Myōkenzan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:suQqqq]][[sn:Nose Myōkenzan]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Myōkenzan]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Myōkenzan]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Myōkenzan]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Myōkenzan]][[Category:qq]] Although technically subordinate to Shinjō-ji, Nose Myōkenzan attracts far more visitors and holds a significant position among Nichiren temples in the Kansai region. Initially managed directly by Shinjō-ji’s priests, it was designated as a detached temple under the {{ill|Religious Organizations Law (Japan)|ja|宗教団体法}} in 1941. Notably, a nearby temple, {{ill|Hontaki-ji|ja|本瀧寺}}, which also enshrines Myōken Bosatsu, is unrelated to Nose Myōkenzan and belongs to the {{ill|Myōken sect|ja|妙見宗}} of the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} school. == History == The origins of the temple date back to the {{ill|Tenpyō-shōhō|ja|天平勝宝}} era (749–757) when the monk Gyōki established {{ill|Daikū-ji|ja|大空寺}} on the summit of Iraku Mountain (now Myōken Mountain).{{Cite web|url=https://smtrc.jp/town-archives/city/toyonaka/p02.html|title=能勢氏ゆかりの日蓮宗霊場「能勢妙見山」|publisher=三井住友トラスト不動産|accessdate=2024-10-23}} During the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, in 986 (Kanna 2), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|ja|源満仲}}, a devout follower of Myōken Bosatsu, relocated an image of {{ill|Chintaku Reifu|lt=Chintaku Reifu Shin|ja|鎮宅霊符神}} (a representation of Myōken) to this site. Later, Mitsunaka’s grandson, {{ill|Minamoto no Yorikuni|ja|源頼国}}, settled in the area, established the Nose clan, and continued to venerate the deity. In 1581 (Tenshō 9), {{ill|Nose Yoritugu|ja|能勢頼次}}, after being defeated by {{ill|Akechi Mitsuhide|ja|明智光秀}}, retreated to Daikū-ji’s remains and built {{ill|Iraku Mountain Castle|ja|為楽山城}}. Following the {{ill|Honnō-ji Incident|ja|本能寺の変}}, Yoritugu joined {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}’s enemies and fled, losing his domain. In 1600 (Keichō 5), Yoritugu regained Nose under {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} and was reinstated as a Tokugawa vassal. Yoritugu became a devout Nichiren follower under {{ill|Nitchi|ja|日乾}}, the 21st chief abbot of {{ill|Minobusan Kuon-ji|ja|身延山久遠寺}}, and established Shinjō-ji in 1603 (Keichō 8). A new Myōken image was carved and enshrined in the newly constructed {{ill|Ka'un-den|ja|開運殿}} on the former castle site, renaming the mountain to Myōken Mountain. == Grounds == The temple features numerous notable structures: * '''Ka'un-den (Main Hall)''' – Rebuilt in 1787 (Tenmei 7) through the patronage of Nose Yoritada. * '''Seirei (Faith Hall)''' – Built in 1998, featuring a glass-floored worship area. * '''Sōshidō (Founder’s Hall)''' – Renovated in 1896. * '''Jōsuidō (Pure Water Hall)''' – Donated by 4th-generation Nakamura Utaemon. * '''Ema-dō (Votive Tablet Hall)''' – Built in 1797 (Kansei 8). * '''Treasure Hall''' – Built in 1922. * '''Statues of Nitchi and Nose Yoritugu'''. == Access == While historically accessible by {{ill|Nose Kaidō|ja|能勢街道}}, modern visitors must rely on private transport or hiking, as public transit access ceased in 2023. Parking is available near the summit, and seasonal traffic restrictions may apply during major events such as New Year’s visits. Shuttle services are provided for worshippers from nearby transit points upon prior arrangement. == Cultural Properties == * '''Natural Monuments (Osaka Prefecture and Kawanishi City):''' ** Beech forest on Myōken Mountain.{{Cite web|url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/guide/spot/8kt5o8vuo9/|title=妙見山のブナ林｜のせでん【能勢電鉄】|accessdate=2024-10-23|publisher=能勢電鉄}} == External Links == * {{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Nose Myōkenzan | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Nose Myokenzan kaiunden.jpg|260px]]
Main Hall | location = 661 Nomachū, Nose-chō, Toyono-gun, Osaka, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34|55|44.328|N|135|27|59.245|E|scale:20000}} | mount = Murozan | sect = Nichiren | deity = Myōken Bosatsu | established = Keichō 8 (1603) | founder = Nitchi | main founder = Nose Yoritugu | alias = Nose Myōkendō
Nose Myōken-gū | official_name = Murozan Shinjō-ji Detached Temple Nose Myōkenzan | related_temples = [[Shinjō-ji (Nose)|Shinjō-ji]] | website = [https://www.myoken.org/ Official Website] }} '''Nose Myōkenzan''' (能勢妙見山, {{lang-en-short|Nose Myōkenzan}}) is a {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|日蓮宗}} temple located in Nose Town, Toyono District, Osaka, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Murozan. The main deity is {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}. Situated near the summit of {{ill|Myōken Mountain (Osaka and Hyōgo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}}, the temple is a revered site for the North Star faith. == Overview == '''Nose Myōkendō''' (能勢妙見堂) or '''Nose Myōken-gū''' (能勢妙見宮), as it is sometimes called, reflects the influence of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合}}, as evidenced by the presence of a {{ill|torii|ja|鳥居}} gate at the temple's entrance.{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.kawanishi.hyogo.jp/dbps_data/_material_/localhost/00test/myoukenryousen.pdf#page=3|title=妙見稜線コース（川西市ハイキングコース案内）|date=2018-03-06|accessdate=2024-10-23}} The temple’s full name is '''Murozan Shinjō-ji Detached Temple Nose Myōkenzan'''.{{Cite web|url=https://www.myoken.org/omen/|title=能勢妙見山の正式name|publisher=日蓮宗霊場 能勢妙見山|accessdate=2024-10-23}} It is a {{ill|detached temple|ja|境外仏堂}} of {{ill|Shinjō-ji (Nose)|ja|真如寺 (大阪府能勢町)|lt=Shinjō-ji}}, also located in Nose Town. Although technically subordinate to Shinjō-ji, Nose Myōkenzan attracts far more visitors and holds a significant position among Nichiren temples in the Kansai region. Initially managed directly by Shinjō-ji’s priests, it was designated as a detached temple under the {{ill|Religious Organizations Law (Japan)|ja|宗教団体法}} in 1941. Notably, a nearby temple, {{ill|Hontaki-ji|ja|本瀧寺}}, which also enshrines Myōken Bosatsu, is unrelated to Nose Myōkenzan and belongs to the {{ill|Myōken sect|ja|妙見宗}} of the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} school. == History == The origins of the temple date back to the {{ill|Tenpyō-shōhō|ja|天平勝宝}} era (749–757) when the monk Gyōki established {{ill|Daikū-ji|ja|大空寺}} on the summit of Iraku Mountain (now Myōken Mountain).{{Cite web|url=https://smtrc.jp/town-archives/city/toyonaka/p02.html|title=能勢氏ゆかりの日蓮宗霊場「能勢妙見山」|publisher=三井住友トラスト不動産|accessdate=2024-10-23}} During the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, in 986 (Kanna 2), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|ja|源満仲}}, a devout follower of Myōken Bosatsu, relocated an image of {{ill|Chintaku Reifu|lt=Chintaku Reifu Shin|ja|鎮宅霊符神}} (a representation of Myōken) to this site. Later, Mitsunaka’s grandson, {{ill|Minamoto no Yorikuni|ja|源頼国}}, settled in the area, established the Nose clan, and continued to venerate the deity. In 1581 (Tenshō 9), {{ill|Nose Yoritugu|ja|能勢頼次}}, after being defeated by {{ill|Akechi Mitsuhide|ja|明智光秀}}, retreated to Daikū-ji’s remains and built {{ill|Iraku Mountain Castle|ja|為楽山城}}. Following the {{ill|Honnō-ji Incident|ja|本能寺の変}}, Yoritugu joined {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}’s enemies and fled, losing his domain. In 1600 (Keichō 5), Yoritugu regained Nose under {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} and was reinstated as a Tokugawa vassal. Yoritugu became a devout Nichiren follower under {{ill|Nitchi|ja|日乾}}, the 21st chief abbot of {{ill|Minobusan Kuon-ji|ja|身延山久遠寺}}, and established Shinjō-ji in 1603 (Keichō 8). A new Myōken image was carved and enshrined in the newly constructed {{ill|Ka'un-den|ja|開運殿}} on the former castle site, renaming the mountain to Myōken Mountain. == Grounds == The temple features numerous notable structures: * '''Ka'un-den (Main Hall)''' – Rebuilt in 1787 (Tenmei 7) through the patronage of Nose Yoritada. * '''Seirei (Faith Hall)''' – Built in 1998, featuring a glass-floored worship area. * '''Sōshidō (Founder’s Hall)''' – Renovated in 1896. * '''Jōsuidō (Pure Water Hall)''' – Donated by 4th-generation Nakamura Utaemon. * '''Ema-dō (Votive Tablet Hall)''' – Built in 1797 (Kansei 8). * '''Treasure Hall''' – Built in 1922. * '''Statues of Nitchi and Nose Yoritugu'''. == Access == While historically accessible by {{ill|Nose Kaidō|ja|能勢街道}}, modern visitors must rely on private transport or hiking, as public transit access ceased in 2023. Parking is available near the summit, and seasonal traffic restrictions may apply during major events such as New Year’s visits. Shuttle services are provided for worshippers from nearby transit points upon prior arrangement. == Cultural Properties == * '''Natural Monuments (Osaka Prefecture and Kawanishi City):''' ** Beech forest on Myōken Mountain.{{Cite web|url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/guide/spot/8kt5o8vuo9/|title=妙見山のブナ林｜のせでん【能勢電鉄】|accessdate=2024-10-23|publisher=能勢電鉄}} == External Links == * [https://www.myoken.org/ Official Website] * {{Instagram|nosemyokenzan}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Nose Myōkenzan}} [[Category:Nose Myōkenzan]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose A new Myōken image was carved and enshrined in the newly constructed {{ill|Ka'un-den|ja|開運殿}} on the former castle site, renaming the mountain to Myōken Mountain. == Grounds == The temple features numerous notable structures: * '''Ka'un-den (Main Hall)''' – Rebuilt in 1787 (Tenmei 7) through the patronage of Nose Yoritada. * '''Seirei (Faith Hall)''' – Built in 1998, featuring a glass-floored worship area. * '''Sōshidō (Founder’s Hall)''' – Renovated in 1896. * '''Jōsuidō (Pure Water Hall)''' – Donated by 4th-generation Nakamura Utaemon. * '''Ema-dō (Votive Tablet Hall)''' – Built in 1797 (Kansei 8). * '''Treasure Hall''' – Built in 1922. * '''Statues of Nitchi and Nose Yoritugu'''. == Access == While historically accessible by {{ill|Nose Kaidō|ja|能勢街道}}, modern visitors must rely on private transport or hiking, as public transit access ceased in 2023. Parking is available near the summit, and seasonal traffic restrictions may apply during major events such as New Year’s visits. Shuttle services are provided for worshippers from nearby transit points upon prior arrangement. == Cultural Properties == * '''Natural Monuments (Osaka Prefecture and Kawanishi City):''' ** Beech forest on Myōken Mountain.{{Cite web|url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/guide/spot/8kt5o8vuo9/|title=妙見山のブナ林｜のせでん【能勢電鉄】|accessdate=2024-10-23|publisher=能勢電鉄}} == External Links == * {{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Nose Myōkenzan | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Nose Myokenzan kaiunden.jpg|260px]]
Main Hall | location = 661 Nomachū, Nose-chō, Toyono-gun, Osaka, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34|55|44.328|N|135|27|59.245|E|scale:20000}} | mount = Murozan | sect = Nichiren | deity = Myōken Bosatsu | established = Keichō 8 (1603) | founder = Nitchi | main founder = Nose Yoritugu | alias = Nose Myōkendō
Nose Myōken-gū | official_name = Murozan Shinjō-ji Detached Temple Nose Myōkenzan | related_temples = [[Shinjō-ji (Nose)|Shinjō-ji]] | website = [https://www.myoken.org/ Official Website] }} '''Nose Myōkenzan''' (能勢妙見山, {{lang-en-short|Nose Myōkenzan}}) is a {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|日蓮宗}} temple located in Nose Town, Toyono District, Osaka, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Murozan. The main deity is {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}. Situated near the summit of {{ill|Myōken Mountain (Osaka and Hyōgo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}}, the temple is a revered site for the North Star faith. == Overview == '''Nose Myōkendō''' (能勢妙見堂) or '''Nose Myōken-gū''' (能勢妙見宮), as it is sometimes called, reflects the influence of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}, as evidenced by the presence of a {{ill|torii|ja|鳥居}} gate at the temple's entrance.{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.kawanishi.hyogo.jp/dbps_data/_material_/localhost/00test/myoukenryousen.pdf#page=3|title=妙見稜線コース（川西市ハイキングコース案内）|date=2018-03-06|accessdate=2024-10-23}} The temple’s full name is '''Murozan Shinjō-ji Detached Temple Nose Myōkenzan'''.{{Cite web|url=https://www.myoken.org/omen/|title=能勢妙見山の正式name|publisher=日蓮宗霊場 能勢妙見山|accessdate=2024-10-23}} It is a {{ill|detached temple|ja|境外仏堂}} of {{ill|Shinjō-ji (Nose)|ja|真如寺 (大阪府能勢町)|lt=Shinjō-ji}}, also located in Nose Town. Although technically subordinate to Shinjō-ji, Nose Myōkenzan attracts far more visitors and holds a significant position among Nichiren temples in the Kansai region. Initially managed directly by Shinjō-ji’s priests, it was designated as a detached temple under the {{ill|Religious Organizations Law (Japan)|ja|宗教団体法}} in 1941. Notably, a nearby temple, {{ill|Hontaki-ji|ja|本瀧寺}}, which also enshrines Myōken Bosatsu, is unrelated to Nose Myōkenzan and belongs to the {{ill|Myōken sect|ja|妙見宗}} of the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} school. == History == The origins of the temple date back to the {{ill|Tenpyō-shōhō|ja|天平勝宝}} era (749–757) when the monk Gyōki established {{ill|Daikū-ji|ja|大空寺}} on the summit of Iraku Mountain (now Myōken Mountain).{{Cite web|url=https://smtrc.jp/town-archives/city/toyonaka/p02.html|title=能勢氏ゆかりの日蓮宗霊場「能勢妙見山」|publisher=三井住友トラスト不動産|accessdate=2024-10-23}} During the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, in 986 (Kanna 2), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|ja|源満仲}}, a devout follower of Myōken Bosatsu, relocated an image of {{ill|Chintaku Reifu|lt=Chintaku Reifu Shin|ja|鎮宅霊符神}} (a representation of Myōken) to this site. Later, Mitsunaka’s grandson, {{ill|Minamoto no Yorikuni|ja|源頼国}}, settled in the area, established the Nose clan, and continued to venerate the deity. In 1581 (Tenshō 9), {{ill|Nose Yoritugu|ja|能勢頼次}}, after being defeated by {{ill|Akechi Mitsuhide|ja|明智光秀}}, retreated to Daikū-ji’s remains and built {{ill|Iraku Mountain Castle|ja|為楽山城}}. Following the {{ill|Honnō-ji Incident|ja|本能寺の変}}, Yoritugu joined {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}’s enemies and fled, losing his domain. In 1600 (Keichō 5), Yoritugu regained Nose under {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} and was reinstated as a Tokugawa vassal. Yoritugu became a devout Nichiren follower under {{ill|Nitchi|ja|日乾}}, the 21st chief abbot of {{ill|Minobusan Kuon-ji|ja|身延山久遠寺}}, and established Shinjō-ji in 1603 (Keichō 8). A new Myōken image was carved and enshrined in the newly constructed {{ill|Ka'un-den|ja|開運殿}} on the former castle site, renaming the mountain to Myōken Mountain. == Grounds == The temple features numerous notable structures: * '''Ka'un-den (Main Hall)''' – Rebuilt in 1787 (Tenmei 7) through the patronage of Nose Yoritada. * '''Seirei (Faith Hall)''' – Built in 1998, featuring a glass-floored worship area. * '''Sōshidō (Founder’s Hall)''' – Renovated in 1896. * '''Jōsuidō (Pure Water Hall)''' – Donated by 4th-generation Nakamura Utaemon. * '''Ema-dō (Votive Tablet Hall)''' – Built in 1797 (Kansei 8). * '''Treasure Hall''' – Built in 1922. * '''Statues of Nitchi and Nose Yoritugu'''. == Access == While historically accessible by {{ill|Nose Kaidō|ja|能勢街道}}, modern visitors must rely on private transport or hiking, as public transit access ceased in 2023. Parking is available near the summit, and seasonal traffic restrictions may apply during major events such as New Year’s visits. Shuttle services are provided for worshippers from nearby transit points upon prior arrangement. == Cultural Properties == * '''Natural Monuments (Osaka Prefecture and Kawanishi City):''' ** Beech forest on Myōken Mountain.{{Cite web|url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/guide/spot/8kt5o8vuo9/|title=妙見山のブナ林｜のせでん【能勢電鉄】|accessdate=2024-10-23|publisher=能勢電鉄}} == External Links == * Nose Myōken-gū | official_name = Murozan Shinjō-ji Detached Temple Nose Myōkenzan | related_temples = [[Shinjō-ji (Nose)|Shinjō-ji]] | website = [https://www.myoken.org/ Official Website] }} '''Nose Myōkenzan''' (能勢妙見山, {{lang-en-short|Nose Myōkenzan}}) is a {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|日蓮宗}} temple located in Nose Town, Toyono District, Osaka, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Murozan. The main deity is {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}. Situated near the summit of {{ill|Myōken Mountain (Osaka and Hyōgo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}}, the temple is a revered site for the North Star faith. == Overview == '''Nose Myōkendō''' (能勢妙見堂) or '''Nose Myōken-gū''' (能勢妙見宮), as it is sometimes called, reflects the influence of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}, as evidenced by the presence of a {{ill|torii|ja|鳥居}} gate at the temple's entrance.{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.kawanishi.hyogo.jp/dbps_data/_material_/localhost/00test/myoukenryousen.pdf#page=3|title=妙見稜線コース（川西市ハイキングコース案内）|date=2018-03-06|accessdate=2024-10-23}} The temple’s full name is '''Murozan Shinjō-ji Detached Temple Nose Myōkenzan'''.{{Cite web|url=https://www.myoken.org/omen/|title=能勢妙見山の正式name|publisher=日蓮宗霊場 能勢妙見山|accessdate=2024-10-23}} It is a {{ill|detached temple|ja|境外仏堂}} of {{ill|Shinjō-ji (Nose)|ja|真如寺 (大阪府能勢町)|lt=Shinjō-ji}}, also located in Nose Town. Although technically subordinate to Shinjō-ji, Nose Myōkenzan attracts far more visitors and holds a significant position among Nichiren temples in the Kansai region. Initially managed directly by Shinjō-ji’s priests, it was designated as a detached temple under the {{ill|Religious Organizations Law (Japan)|ja|宗教団体法}} in 1941. Notably, a nearby temple, {{ill|Hontaki-ji|ja|本瀧寺}}, which also enshrines Myōken Bosatsu, is unrelated to Nose Myōkenzan and belongs to the {{ill|Myōken sect|ja|妙見宗}} of the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} school. == History == The origins of the temple date back to the {{ill|Tenpyō-shōhō|ja|天平勝宝}} era (749–757) when the monk Gyōki established {{ill|Daikū-ji|ja|大空寺}} on the summit of Iraku Mountain (now Myōken Mountain).{{Cite web|url=https://smtrc.jp/town-archives/city/toyonaka/p02.html|title=能勢氏ゆかりの日蓮宗霊場「能勢妙見山」|publisher=三井住友トラスト不動産|accessdate=2024-10-23}} During the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, in 986 (Kanna 2), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|ja|源満仲}}, a devout follower of Myōken Bosatsu, relocated an image of {{ill|Chintaku Reifu|lt=Chintaku Reifu Shin|ja|鎮宅霊符神}} (a representation of Myōken) to this site. Later, Mitsunaka’s grandson, {{ill|Minamoto no Yorikuni|ja|源頼国}}, settled in the area, established the Nose clan, and continued to venerate the deity. In 1581 (Tenshō 9), {{ill|Nose Yoritugu|ja|能勢頼次}}, after being defeated by {{ill|Akechi Mitsuhide|ja|明智光秀}}, retreated to Daikū-ji’s remains and built {{ill|Iraku Mountain Castle|ja|為楽山城}}. Following the {{ill|Honnō-ji Incident|ja|本能寺の変}}, Yoritugu joined {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}’s enemies and fled, losing his domain. In 1600 (Keichō 5), Yoritugu regained Nose under {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} and was reinstated as a Tokugawa vassal. Yoritugu became a devout Nichiren follower under {{ill|Nitchi|ja|日乾}}, the 21st chief abbot of {{ill|Minobusan Kuon-ji|ja|身延山久遠寺}}, and established Shinjō-ji in 1603 (Keichō 8). A new Myōken image was carved and enshrined in the newly constructed {{ill|Ka'un-den|ja|開運殿}} on the former castle site, renaming the mountain to Myōken Mountain. == Grounds == The temple features numerous notable structures: * '''Ka'un-den (Main Hall)''' – Rebuilt in 1787 (Tenmei 7) through the patronage of Nose Yoritada. * '''Seirei (Faith Hall)''' – Built in 1998, featuring a glass-floored worship area. * '''Sōshidō (Founder’s Hall)''' – Renovated in 1896. * '''Jōsuidō (Pure Water Hall)''' – Donated by 4th-generation Nakamura Utaemon. * '''Ema-dō (Votive Tablet Hall)''' – Built in 1797 (Kansei 8). * '''Treasure Hall''' – Built in 1922. * '''Statues of Nitchi and Nose Yoritugu'''. == Access == While historically accessible by {{ill|Nose Kaidō|ja|能勢街道}}, modern visitors must rely on private transport or hiking, as public transit access ceased in 2023. Parking is available near the summit, and seasonal traffic restrictions may apply during major events such as New Year’s visits. Shuttle services are provided for worshippers from nearby transit points upon prior arrangement. == Cultural Properties == * '''Natural Monuments (Osaka Prefecture and Kawanishi City):''' ** Beech forest on Myōken Mountain.{{Cite web|url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/guide/spot/8kt5o8vuo9/|title=妙見山のブナ林｜のせでん【能勢電鉄】|accessdate=2024-10-23|publisher=能勢電鉄}} == External Links == * {{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Nose Myōkenzan | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Nose Myokenzan kaiunden.jpg|260px]]
Main Hall | location = 661 Nomachū, Nose-chō, Toyono-gun, Osaka, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34|55|44.328|N|135|27|59.245|E|scale:20000}} | mount = Murozan | sect = Nichiren | deity = Myōken Bosatsu | established = Keichō 8 (1603) | founder = Nikken | main founder = Nose Yoritugu | alias = Nose Myōkendō
Nose Myōken-gū | official_name = Murozan Shinjō-ji Detached Temple Nose Myōkenzan | related_temples = [[Shinjō-ji (Nose)|Shinjō-ji]] | website = [https://www.myoken.org/ Official Website] }} '''Nose Myōkenzan''' (能勢妙見山, {{lang-en-short|Nose Myōkenzan}}) is a {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|Nichiren宗}} temple located in Nose Town, Toyono District, Osaka, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Murozan. The main deity is {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}. Situated near the summit of {{ill|Myōken Mountain (Osaka and Hyōgo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}}, the temple is a revered site for the North Star faith. == Overview == '''Nose Myōkendō''' (能勢妙見堂) or '''Nose Myōken-gū''' (能勢妙見宮), as it is sometimes called, reflects the influence of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}, as evidenced by the presence of a {{ill|torii|ja|鳥居}} gate at the temple's entrance.{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.kawanishi.hyogo.jp/dbps_data/_material_/localhost/00test/myoukenryousen.pdf#page=3|title=妙見稜線コース（川西市ハイキングコース案内）|date=2018-03-06|accessdate=2024-10-23}} The temple’s full name is '''Murozan Shinjō-ji Detached Temple Nose Myōkenzan'''.{{Cite web|url=https://www.myoken.org/omen/|title=能勢妙見山の正式name|publisher=Nichiren宗霊場 能勢妙見山|accessdate=2024-10-23}} It is a {{ill|detached temple|ja|境外仏堂}} of {{ill|Shinjō-ji (Nose)|ja|真如寺 (大阪府能勢町)|lt=Shinjō-ji}}, also located in Nose Town. Although technically subordinate to Shinjō-ji, Nose Myōkenzan attracts far more visitors and holds a significant position among Nichiren temples in the Kansai region. Initially managed directly by Shinjō-ji’s priests, it was designated as a detached temple under the {{ill|Religious Organizations Law (Japan)|ja|宗教団体法}} in 1941. Notably, a nearby temple, {{ill|Hontaki-ji|ja|本瀧寺}}, which also enshrines Myōken Bosatsu, is unrelated to Nose Myōkenzan and belongs to the {{ill|Myōken sect|ja|妙見宗}} of the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} school. == History == The origins of the temple date back to the {{ill|Tenpyō-shōhō|ja|天平勝宝}} era (749–757) when the monk Gyōki established {{ill|Daikū-ji|ja|大空寺}} on the summit of Iraku Mountain (now Myōken Mountain).{{Cite web|url=https://smtrc.jp/town-archives/city/toyonaka/p02.html|title=能勢氏ゆかりのNichiren宗霊場「能勢妙見山」|publisher=三井住友トラスト不動産|accessdate=2024-10-23}} During the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, in 986 (Kanna 2), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|ja|源満仲}}, a devout follower of Myōken Bosatsu, relocated an image of {{ill|Chintaku Reifu|lt=Chintaku Reifu Shin|ja|鎮宅霊符神}} (a representation of Myōken) to this site. Later, Mitsunaka’s grandson, {{ill|Minamoto no Yorikuni|ja|源頼国}}, settled in the area, established the Nose clan, and continued to venerate the deity. In 1581 (Tenshō 9), {{ill|Nose Yoritugu|ja|能勢頼次}}, after being defeated by {{ill|Akechi Mitsuhide|ja|明智光秀}}, retreated to Daikū-ji’s remains and built {{ill|Iraku Mountain Castle|ja|為楽山城}}. Following the {{ill|Honnō-ji Incident|ja|本能寺の変}}, Yoritugu joined {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}’s enemies and fled, losing his domain. In 1600 (Keichō 5), Yoritugu regained Nose under {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} and was reinstated as a Tokugawa vassal. Yoritugu became a devout Nichiren follower under {{ill|Nikken|ja|日乾}}, the 21st chief abbot of {{ill|Minobusan Kuon-ji|ja|身延山久遠寺}}, and established Shinjō-ji in 1603 (Keichō 8). A new Myōken image was carved and enshrined in the newly constructed {{ill|Ka'un-den|ja|開運殿}} on the former castle site, renaming the mountain to Myōken Mountain. == Grounds == The temple features numerous notable structures: * '''Ka'un-den (Main Hall)''' – Rebuilt in 1787 (Tenmei 7) through the patronage of Nose Yoritada. * '''Seirei (Faith Hall)''' – Built in 1998, featuring a glass-floored worship area. * '''Sōshidō (Founder’s Hall)''' – Renovated in 1896. * '''Jōsuidō (Pure Water Hall)''' – Donated by 4th-generation Nakamura Utaemon. * '''Ema-dō (Votive Tablet Hall)''' – Built in 1797 (Kansei 8). * '''Treasure Hall''' – Built in 1922. * '''Statues of Nikken and Nose Yoritugu'''. == Access == While historically accessible by {{ill|Nose Kaidō|ja|能勢街道}}, modern visitors must rely on private transport or hiking, as public transit access ceased in 2023. Parking is available near the summit, and seasonal traffic restrictions may apply during major events such as New Year’s visits. Shuttle services are provided for worshippers from nearby transit points upon prior arrangement. == Cultural Properties == * '''Natural Monuments (Osaka Prefecture and Kawanishi City):''' ** Beech forest on Myōken Mountain.{{Cite web|url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/guide/spot/8kt5o8vuo9/|title=妙見山のブナ林｜のせでん【能勢電鉄】|accessdate=2024-10-23|publisher=能勢電鉄}} == External Links == * Notably, a nearby temple, {{ill|Hontaki-ji|ja|本瀧寺}}, which also enshrines Myōken Bosatsu, is unrelated to Nose Myōkenzan and belongs to the {{ill|Myōken sect|ja|妙見宗}} of the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} school. == History == The origins of the temple date back to the {{ill|Tenpyō-shōhō|ja|天平勝宝}} era (749–757) when the monk Gyōki established {{ill|Daikū-ji|ja|大空寺}} on the summit of Iraku Mountain (now Myōken Mountain).{{Cite web|url=https://smtrc.jp/town-archives/city/toyonaka/p02.html|title=能勢氏ゆかりのNichiren宗霊場「能勢妙見山」|publisher=三井住友トラスト不動産|accessdate=2024-10-23}} During the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, in 986 (Kanna 2), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|ja|源満仲}}, a devout follower of Myōken Bosatsu, relocated an image of {{ill|Chintaku Reifu|lt=Chintaku Reifu Shin|ja|鎮宅霊符神}} (a representation of Myōken) to this site. Later, Mitsunaka’s grandson, {{ill|Minamoto no Yorikuni|ja|源頼国}}, settled in the area, established the Nose clan, and continued to venerate the deity. In 1581 (Tenshō 9), {{ill|Nose Yoritugu|ja|能勢頼次}}, after being defeated by {{ill|Akechi Mitsuhide|ja|明智光秀}}, retreated to Daikū-ji’s remains and built {{ill|Iraku Mountain Castle|ja|為楽山城}}. Following the {{ill|Honnō-ji Incident|ja|本能寺の変}}, Yoritugu joined {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}’s enemies and fled, losing his domain. In 1600 (Keichō 5), Yoritugu regained Nose under {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} and was reinstated as a Tokugawa vassal. Yoritugu became a devout Nichiren follower under {{ill|Nikken|ja|日乾}}, the 21st chief abbot of {{ill|Minobusan Kuon-ji|ja|身延山久遠寺}}, and established Shinjō-ji in 1603 (Keichō 8). A new Myōken image was carved and enshrined in the newly constructed {{ill|Ka'un-den|ja|開運殿}} on the former castle site, renaming the mountain to Myōken Mountain. == Grounds == The temple features numerous notable structures: * '''Ka'un-den (Main Hall)''' – Rebuilt in 1787 (Tenmei 7) through the patronage of Nose Yoritada. * '''Seirei (Faith Hall)''' – Built in 1998, featuring a glass-floored worship area. * '''Sōshidō (Founder’s Hall)''' – Renovated in 1896. * '''Jōsuidō (Pure Water Hall)''' – Donated by 4th-generation Nakamura Utaemon. * '''Ema-dō (Votive Tablet Hall)''' – Built in 1797 (Kansei 8). * '''Treasure Hall''' – Built in 1922. * '''Statues of Nikken and Nose Yoritugu'''. == Access == While historically accessible by {{ill|Nose Kaidō|ja|能勢街道}}, modern visitors must rely on private transport or hiking, as public transit access ceased in 2023. Parking is available near the summit, and seasonal traffic restrictions may apply during major events such as New Year’s visits. Shuttle services are provided for worshippers from nearby transit points upon prior arrangement. == Cultural Properties == * '''Natural Monuments (Osaka Prefecture and Kawanishi City):''' ** Beech forest on Myōken Mountain.{{Cite web|url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/guide/spot/8kt5o8vuo9/|title=妙見山のブナ林｜のせでん【能勢電鉄】|accessdate=2024-10-23|publisher=能勢電鉄}} == External Links == * {{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Nose Myōkenzan | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Nose Myokenzan kaiunden.jpg|260px]]
Main Hall | location = 661 Nomachū, Nose-chō, Toyono-gun, Osaka, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34|55|44.328|N|135|27|59.245|E|scale:20000}} | mount = Murozan | sect = Nichiren | deity = Myōken Bosatsu | established = Keichō 8 (1603) | founder = Nikken | main founder = Nose Yoritugu | alias = Nose Myōkendō
Nose Myōken-gū | official_name = Murozan Shinjō-ji Detached Temple Nose Myōkenzan | related_temples = [[Shinjō-ji (Nose)|Shinjō-ji]] | website = [https://www.myoken.org/ Official Website] }} '''Nose Myōkenzan''' (能勢妙見山, {{lang-en-short|Nose Myōkenzan}}) is a {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|Nichiren宗}} temple located in Nose Town, Toyono District, Osaka, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Murozan. The main deity is {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}. Situated near the summit of {{ill|Myōken Mountain (Osaka and Hyōgo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}}, the temple is a revered site for the North Star faith. == Overview == '''Nose Myōkendō''' (能勢妙見堂) or '''Nose Myōken-gū''' (能勢妙見宮), as it is sometimes called, reflects the influence of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}, as evidenced by the presence of a {{ill|torii|ja|鳥居}} gate at the temple's entrance.{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.kawanishi.hyogo.jp/dbps_data/_material_/localhost/00test/myoukenryousen.pdf#page=3|title=妙見稜線コース（川西市ハイキングコース案内）|date=2018-03-06|accessdate=2024-10-23}} The temple’s full name is '''Murozan Shinjō-ji Detached Temple Nose Myōkenzan'''.{{Cite web|url=https://www.myoken.org/omen/|title=能勢妙見山の正式name|publisher=Nichiren宗霊場 能勢妙見山|accessdate=2024-10-23}} It is a {{ill|detached temple|ja|境外仏堂}} of {{ill|Shinjō-ji (Nose)|ja|真如寺 (大阪府能勢町)|lt=Shinjō-ji}}, also located in Nose Town. Although technically subordinate to Shinjō-ji, Nose Myōkenzan attracts far more visitors and holds a significant position among Nichiren temples in the Kansai region. Initially managed directly by Shinjō-ji’s priests, it was designated as a detached temple under the {{ill|Religious Organizations Law (Japan)|ja|宗教団体法}} in 1941. Notably, a nearby temple, {{ill|Hontaki-ji|ja|本瀧寺}}, which also enshrines Myōken Bosatsu, is unrelated to Nose Myōkenzan and belongs to the {{ill|Myōken sect|ja|妙見宗}} of the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} school. == History == The origins of the temple date back to the {{ill|Tenpyō-shōhō|ja|天平勝宝}} era (749–757) when the monk Gyōki established {{ill|Daikū-ji|ja|大空寺}} on the summit of Iraku Mountain (now Myōken Mountain).{{Cite web|url=https://smtrc.jp/town-archives/city/toyonaka/p02.html|title=能勢氏ゆかりのNichiren宗霊場「能勢妙見山」|publisher=三井住友トラスト不動産|accessdate=2024-10-23}} During the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, in 986 (Kanna 2), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|ja|源満仲}}, a devout follower of Myōken Bosatsu, relocated an image of {{ill|Chintaku Reifu|lt=Chintaku Reifu Shin|ja|鎮宅霊符神}} (a representation of Myōken) to this site. Later, Mitsunaka’s grandson, {{ill|Minamoto no Yorikuni|ja|源頼国}}, settled in the area, established the Nose clan, and continued to venerate the deity. In 1581 (Tenshō 9), {{ill|Nose Yoritugu|ja|能勢頼次}}, after being defeated by {{ill|Akechi Mitsuhide|ja|明智光秀}}, retreated to Daikū-ji’s remains and built {{ill|Iraku Mountain Castle|ja|為楽山城}}. Following the {{ill|Honnō-ji Incident|ja|本能寺の変}}, Yoritugu joined {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}’s enemies and fled, losing his domain. In 1600 (Keichō 5), Yoritugu regained Nose under {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} and was reinstated as a Tokugawa vassal. Yoritugu became a devout Nichiren follower under {{ill|Nikken|ja|日乾}}, the 21st chief abbot of {{ill|Minobusan Kuon-ji|ja|身延山久遠寺}}, and established Shinjō-ji in 1603 (Keichō 8). A new Myōken image was carved and enshrined in the newly constructed {{ill|Ka'un-den|ja|開運殿}} on the former castle site, renaming the mountain to Myōken Mountain. == Grounds == The temple features numerous notable structures: * '''Ka'un-den (Main Hall)''' – Rebuilt in 1787 (Tenmei 7) through the patronage of Nose Yoritada. * '''Seirei (Faith Hall)''' – Built in 1998, featuring a glass-floored worship area. * '''Sōshidō (Founder’s Hall)''' – Renovated in 1896. * '''Jōsuidō (Pure Water Hall)''' – Donated by 4th-generation Nakamura Utaemon. * '''Ema-dō (Votive Tablet Hall)''' – Built in 1797 (Kansei 8). * '''Treasure Hall''' – Built in 1922. * '''Statues of Nikken and Nose Yoritugu'''. == Access == While historically accessible by {{ill|Nose Kaidō|ja|能勢街道}}, modern visitors must rely on private transport or hiking, as public transit access ceased in 2023. Parking is available near the summit, and seasonal traffic restrictions may apply during major events such as New Year’s visits. Shuttle services are provided for worshippers from nearby transit points upon prior arrangement. == Cultural Properties == * '''Natural Monuments (Osaka Prefecture and Kawanishi City):''' ** Beech forest on Myōken Mountain.{{Cite web|url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/guide/spot/8kt5o8vuo9/|title=妙見山のブナ林｜のせでん【能勢電鉄】|accessdate=2024-10-23|publisher=能勢電鉄}} == External Links == * A new Myōken image was carved and enshrined in the newly constructed {{ill|Ka'un-den|ja|開運殿}} on the former castle site, renaming the mountain to Myōken Mountain. == Grounds == The temple features numerous notable structures: * '''Ka'un-den (Main Hall)''' – Rebuilt in 1787 (Tenmei 7) through the patronage of Nose Yoritada. * '''Seirei (Faith Hall)''' – Built in 1998, featuring a glass-floored worship area. * '''Sōshidō (Founder’s Hall)''' – Renovated in 1896. * '''Jōsuidō (Pure Water Hall)''' – Donated by 4th-generation Nakamura Utaemon. * '''Ema-dō (Votive Tablet Hall)''' – Built in 1797 (Kansei 8). * '''Treasure Hall''' – Built in 1922. * '''Statues of Nikken and Nose Yoritugu'''. == Access == While historically accessible by {{ill|Nose Kaidō|ja|能勢街道}}, modern visitors must rely on private transport or hiking, as public transit access ceased in 2023. Parking is available near the summit, and seasonal traffic restrictions may apply during major events such as New Year’s visits. Shuttle services are provided for worshippers from nearby transit points upon prior arrangement. == Cultural Properties == * '''Natural Monuments (Osaka Prefecture and Kawanishi City):''' ** Beech forest on Myōken Mountain.{{Cite web|url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/guide/spot/8kt5o8vuo9/|title=妙見山のブナ林｜のせでん【能勢電鉄】|accessdate=2024-10-23|publisher=能勢電鉄}} == External Links == * {{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Nose Myōkenzan | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Nose Myokenzan kaiunden.jpg|260px]]
Main Hall | location = 661 Nomachū, Nose-chō, Toyono-gun, Osaka, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34|55|44.328|N|135|27|59.245|E|scale:20000}} | mount = Murozan | sect = Nichiren | deity = Myōken Bosatsu | established = Keichō 8 (1603) | founder = Nikken | main founder = Nose Yoritugu | alias = Nose Myōkendō
Nose Myōken-gū | official_name = Murozan Shinjō-ji Detached Temple Nose Myōkenzan | related_temples = [[Shinjō-ji (Nose)|Shinjō-ji]] | website = [https://www.myoken.org/ Official Website] }} '''Nose Myōkenzan''' (能勢妙見山, {{lang-en-short|Nose Myōkenzan}}) is a {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|Nichiren宗}} temple located in Nose Town, Toyono District, Osaka, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Murozan. The main deity is {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}. Situated near the summit of {{ill|Myōken Mountain (Osaka and Hyōgo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}}, the temple is a revered site for the North Star faith. == Overview == '''Nose Myōkendō''' (能勢妙見堂) or '''Nose Myōken-gū''' (能勢妙見宮), as it is sometimes called, reflects the influence of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}, as evidenced by the presence of a {{ill|torii|ja|鳥居}} gate at the temple's entrance.{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.kawanishi.hyogo.jp/dbps_data/_material_/localhost/00test/myoukenryousen.pdf#page=3|title=妙見稜線コース（川西市ハイキングコース案内）|date=2018-03-06|accessdate=2024-10-23}} The temple’s full name is '''Murozan Shinjō-ji Detached Temple Nose Myōkenzan'''.{{Cite web|url=https://www.myoken.org/omen/|title=能勢妙見山の正式name|publisher=Nichiren宗霊場 能勢妙見山|accessdate=2024-10-23}} It is a {{ill|detached temple|ja|境外仏堂}} of {{ill|Shinjō-ji (Nose)|ja|真如寺 (大阪府能勢町)|lt=Shinjō-ji}}, also located in Nose Town. Although technically subordinate to Shinjō-ji, Nose Myōkenzan attracts far more visitors and holds a significant position among Nichiren temples in the Kansai region. Initially managed directly by Shinjō-ji’s priests, it was designated as a detached temple under the {{ill|Religious Organizations Law (Japan)|ja|宗教団体法}} in 1941. Notably, a nearby temple, {{ill|Hontaki-ji|ja|本瀧寺}}, which also enshrines Myōken Bosatsu, is unrelated to Nose Myōkenzan and belongs to the {{ill|Myōken sect|ja|妙見宗}} of the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} school. == History == The origins of the temple date back to the {{ill|Tenpyō-shōhō|ja|天平勝宝}} era (749–757) when the monk Gyōki established {{ill|Daikū-ji|ja|大空寺}} on the summit of Iraku Mountain (now Myōken Mountain).{{Cite web|url=https://smtrc.jp/town-archives/city/toyonaka/p02.html|title=能勢氏ゆかりのNichiren宗霊場「能勢妙見山」|publisher=三井住友トラスト不動産|accessdate=2024-10-23}} During the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, in 986 (Kanna 2), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|ja|源満仲}}, a devout follower of Myōken Bosatsu, relocated an image of {{ill|Chintaku Reifu|lt=Chintaku Reifu Shin|ja|鎮宅霊符神}} (a representation of Myōken) to this site. Later, Mitsunaka’s grandson, {{ill|Minamoto no Yorikuni|ja|源頼国}}, settled in the area, established the Nose clan, and continued to venerate the deity. In 1581 (Tenshō 9), {{ill|Nose Yoritugu|ja|能勢頼次}}, after being defeated by {{ill|Akechi Mitsuhide|ja|明智光秀}}, retreated to Daikū-ji’s remains and built {{ill|Iraku Mountain Castle|ja|為楽山城}}. Following the {{ill|Honnō-ji Incident|ja|本能寺の変}}, Yoritugu joined {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}’s enemies and fled, losing his domain. In 1600 (Keichō 5), Yoritugu regained Nose under {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} and was reinstated as a Tokugawa vassal. Yoritugu became a devout Nichiren follower under {{ill|Nikken|ja|日乾}}, the 21st chief abbot of {{ill|Minobusan Kuon-ji|ja|身延山久遠寺}}, and established Shinjō-ji in 1603 (Keichō 8). A new Myōken image was carved and enshrined in the newly constructed {{ill|Ka'un-den|ja|開運殿}} on the former castle site, renaming the mountain to Myōken Mountain. == Grounds == The temple features numerous notable structures: * '''Ka'un-den (Main Hall)''' – Rebuilt in 1787 (Tenmei 7) through the patronage of Nose Yoritada. * '''Seirei (Faith Hall)''' – Built in 1998, featuring a glass-floored worship area. * '''Sōshidō (Founder’s Hall)''' – Renovated in 1896. * '''Jōsuidō (Pure Water Hall)''' – Donated by 4th-generation Nakamura Utaemon. * '''Ema-dō (Votive Tablet Hall)''' – Built in 1797 (Kansei 8). * '''Treasure Hall''' – Built in 1922. * '''Statues of Nikken and Nose Yoritugu'''. == Access == While historically accessible by {{ill|Nose Kaidō|ja|能勢街道}}, modern visitors must rely on private transport or hiking, as public transit access ceased in 2023. Parking is available near the summit, and seasonal traffic restrictions may apply during major events such as New Year’s visits. Shuttle services are provided for worshippers from nearby transit points upon prior arrangement. == Cultural Properties == * '''Natural Monuments (Osaka Prefecture and Kawanishi City):''' ** Beech forest on Myōken Mountain.{{Cite web|url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/guide/spot/8kt5o8vuo9/|title=妙見山のブナ林｜のせでん【能勢電鉄】|accessdate=2024-10-23|publisher=能勢電鉄}} == External Links == * Nose Myōken-gū | official_name = Murozan Shinjō-ji Detached Temple Nose Myōkenzan | related_temples = [[Shinjō-ji (Nose)|Shinjō-ji]] | website = [https://www.myoken.org/ Official Website] }} '''Nose Myōkenzan''' (能勢妙見山, {{lang-en-short|Nose Myōkenzan}}) is a {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|Nichiren宗}} temple located in Nose Town, Toyono District, Osaka, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Murozan. The main deity is {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}. Situated near the summit of {{ill|Myōken Mountain (Osaka and Hyōgo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}}, the temple is a revered site for the North Star faith. == Overview == '''Nose Myōkendō''' (能勢妙見堂) or '''Nose Myōken-gū''' (能勢妙見宮), as it is sometimes called, reflects the influence of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}, as evidenced by the presence of a {{ill|torii|ja|鳥居}} gate at the temple's entrance.{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.kawanishi.hyogo.jp/dbps_data/_material_/localhost/00test/myoukenryousen.pdf#page=3|title=妙見稜線コース（川西市ハイキングコース案内）|date=2018-03-06|accessdate=2024-10-23}} The temple’s full name is '''Murozan Shinjō-ji Detached Temple Nose Myōkenzan'''.{{Cite web|url=https://www.myoken.org/omen/|title=能勢妙見山の正式name|publisher=Nichiren宗霊場 能勢妙見山|accessdate=2024-10-23}} It is a {{ill|detached temple|ja|境外仏堂}} of {{ill|Shinjō-ji (Nose)|ja|真如寺 (大阪府能勢町)|lt=Shinjō-ji}}, also located in Nose Town. Although technically subordinate to Shinjō-ji, Nose Myōkenzan attracts far more visitors and holds a significant position among Nichiren temples in the Kansai region. Initially managed directly by Shinjō-ji’s priests, it was designated as a detached temple under the {{ill|Religious Organizations Law (Japan)|ja|宗教団体法}} in 1941. Notably, a nearby temple, {{ill|Hontaki-ji|ja|本瀧寺}}, which also enshrines Myōken Bosatsu, is unrelated to Nose Myōkenzan and belongs to the {{ill|Myōken sect|ja|妙見宗}} of the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} school. == History == The origins of the temple date back to the {{ill|Tenpyō-shōhō|ja|天平勝宝}} era (749–757) when the monk Gyōki established {{ill|Daikū-ji|ja|大空寺}} on the summit of Iraku Mountain (now Myōken Mountain).{{Cite web|url=https://smtrc.jp/town-archives/city/toyonaka/p02.html|title=能勢氏ゆかりのNichiren宗霊場「能勢妙見山」|publisher=三井住友トラスト不動産|accessdate=2024-10-23}} During the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, in 986 (Kanna 2), {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|ja|源満仲}}, a devout follower of Myōken Bosatsu, relocated an image of {{ill|Chintaku Reifu|lt=Chintaku Reifu Shin|ja|鎮宅霊符神}} (a representation of Myōken) to this site. Later, Mitsunaka’s grandson, {{ill|Minamoto no Yorikuni|ja|源頼国}}, settled in the area, established the Nose clan, and continued to venerate the deity. In 1581 (Tenshō 9), {{ill|Nose Yoritugu|ja|能勢頼次}}, after being defeated by {{ill|Akechi Mitsuhide|ja|明智光秀}}, retreated to Daikū-ji’s remains and built {{ill|Iraku Mountain Castle|ja|為楽山城}}. Following the {{ill|Honnō-ji Incident|ja|本能寺の変}}, Yoritugu joined {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}’s enemies and fled, losing his domain. In 1600 (Keichō 5), Yoritugu regained Nose under {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} and was reinstated as a Tokugawa vassal. Yoritugu became a devout Nichiren follower under {{ill|Nikken|ja|日乾}}, the 21st chief abbot of {{ill|Minobusan Kuon-ji|ja|身延山久遠寺}}, and established Shinjō-ji in 1603 (Keichō 8). A new Myōken image was carved and enshrined in the newly constructed {{ill|Ka'un-den|ja|開運殿}} on the former castle site, renaming the mountain to Myōken Mountain. == Grounds == The temple features numerous notable structures: * '''Ka'un-den (Main Hall)''' – Rebuilt in 1787 (Tenmei 7) through the patronage of Nose Yoritada. * '''Seirei (Faith Hall)''' – Built in 1998, featuring a glass-floored worship area. * '''Sōshidō (Founder’s Hall)''' – Renovated in 1896. * '''Jōsuidō (Pure Water Hall)''' – Donated by 4th-generation Nakamura Utaemon. * '''Ema-dō (Votive Tablet Hall)''' – Built in 1797 (Kansei 8). * '''Treasure Hall''' – Built in 1922. * '''Statues of Nikken and Nose Yoritugu'''. == Access == While historically accessible by {{ill|Nose Kaidō|ja|能勢街道}}, modern visitors must rely on private transport or hiking, as public transit access ceased in 2023. Parking is available near the summit, and seasonal traffic restrictions may apply during major events such as New Year’s visits. Shuttle services are provided for worshippers from nearby transit points upon prior arrangement. == Cultural Properties == * '''Natural Monuments (Osaka Prefecture and Kawanishi City):''' ** Beech forest on Myōken Mountain.{{Cite web|url=https://noseden.hankyu.co.jp/guide/spot/8kt5o8vuo9/|title=妙見山のブナ林｜のせでん【能勢電鉄】|accessdate=2024-10-23|publisher=能勢電鉄}} == External Links == *
